target.title: fix(config): allow historyLimit: 0 in GroupChatSchema
target.body:
## Summary

`GroupChatSchema.historyLimit` used `.positive()`, which rejects `0`, while the other `historyLimit` schemas accept `0` with `.min(0)`. This made `messages.groupChat.historyLimit: 0` invalid even though `0` is the existing config value for disabling history.

Fixes #65305

## Fix

- Change `messages.groupChat.historyLimit` from `.positive()` to `.min(0)` in `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts`.
- Add focused runtime config schema tests for `0`, positive values, and negative values.
- Add a Gateway `config.patch` regression test proving the runtime API path accepts `historyLimit: 0`.

## Current Status

- Rebased cleanly on current `upstream/main` (`33b81686ad`).
- Current PR head is `a4f99c4cd2c05c26e52a06b315a5b81da24f509b`.
- Diff against current main is one commit: `git rev-list --left-right --count upstream/main...refresh-historylimit-zero` -> `0 1`.
- Main has not already fixed this: current main still has `.positive()` for `GroupChatSchema.historyLimit`.

## Test Plan

Current head verification after the final rebase:

- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/zod-schema.core.test.ts`
- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts -t "historyLimit: 0"`
- [x] `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/config/zod-schema.core.ts src/config/zod-schema.core.test.ts src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts`
- [x] `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `messages.groupChat.historyLimit: 0` must be accepted as a valid runtime config value so group chat history can be disabled.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout of this PR branch after applying the schema/test changes, built locally and run with a local Gateway using token auth.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built the branch with `OPENCLAW_RUN_NODE_SKIP_DTS_BUILD=1 pnpm build`, started the built local Gateway, connected through the backend Gateway protocol with `operator.admin`, read the runtime config schema, called `config.patch`, and read the config back from the running server.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the local Gateway API smoke run:

```text
helloAuth.scopes: ["operator.admin"]
schema.messages.groupChat.historyLimit: { type: "integer", minimum: 0 }
before: { valid: true, historyLimit: 1 }
config.patch: { ok: true, noop: false, historyLimit: 0 }
after: { valid: true, historyLimit: 0 }
```

**Observed result after fix:** The running Gateway advertised `minimum: 0` for `messages.groupChat.historyLimit`; `config.patch` accepted `historyLimit: 0` with `ok: true`; a follow-up read returned `valid: true` and `historyLimit: 0`.

**What was not tested:** The dashboard browser clickpath was not exercised; this proof targets the runtime Gateway/API config validation path directly.

This specifically covers the concern that direct config loading may accept a value while runtime JSON Schema/API validation may still reject it.

target.title: fix(gateway): cap authenticated websocket identities
target.body:
## Summary
- Cap authenticated gateway websocket sessions per device identity instead of moving every successful handshake into an unbounded shared client set.
- Keep device-less authenticated sessions inside the existing pre-auth budget so shared-auth clients cannot bypass connection limits by omitting device identity.

## Changes
- Added an authenticated connection budget helper with an env-configurable limit and device-scoped acquire/release tracking.
- Enforced the budget during websocket `setClient`, rejecting excess authenticated connections with a policy close before they enter the active client set.
- Added regression coverage for device-scoped limits, budget-slot reclaim after disconnect, device-less authenticated sessions staying under the pre-auth cap, and invalid env fallback handling.

## Validation
- Ran `corepack pnpm test -- src/gateway/server/authenticated-connection-budget.test.ts src/gateway/server.preauth-hardening.test.ts`
- Ran `PATH="/tmp/corepack-bin:$PATH" corepack pnpm build`
- Ran `pnpm check` via the scoped commit hook during both commits
- Ran local agentic review with `claude -p` against the patch/commit and addressed the actionable feedback

## Notes
- Default authenticated connection budget is `8` per device identity, with `OPENCLAW_MAX_AUTHENTICATED_CONNECTIONS_PER_IDENTITY` and `OPENCLAW_TEST_MAX_AUTHENTICATED_CONNECTIONS_PER_IDENTITY` overrides.
- Residual risk: device-less authenticated sessions still rely on the existing pre-auth budget semantics, so their ceiling remains tied to the pre-auth per-IP budget rather than the new device budget.

target.title: fix(memory-host): add AbortSignal support to batch polling (OpenAI, Voyage, Gemini)
target.body:
Add AbortSignal support to batch polling loops to enable immediate cancellation when clients disconnect.

Problem
When HTTP clients disconnect during batch embedding operations, server-side polling continues until timeout, wasting API quota.

Solution
- Replace uncancelable setTimeout with cancellable version using clearTimeout
- Add signal?: AbortSignal parameter to all batch providers
- Use { once: true } to prevent listener accumulation (fixes MaxListenersExceededWarning)
- Thread signal through full call chain: batch-runner → runXxxBatches → waitForXxxBatch

Changes
- batch-runner.ts: Add signal to EmbeddingBatchExecutionParams type
- batch-openai.ts: Add signal support with {once: true} cleanup
- batch-voyage.ts: Update sleep type, add signal threading through deps
- batch-gemini.ts: Add signal support with {once: true} cleanup

Testing
- Verified polling stops within 1s of abort (was 30+ iterations)
- No listener leaks after long polls
- TypeScript compiles without errors

Backwards Compatible: signal is optional.

**Additional Fixes (Post-Review)**:
- Fixed `initial: batchInfo` parameter regression in `batch-voyage.ts` and `batch-gemini.ts` to avoid extra API calls on batch creation
- Fixed locale regression in `ui/src/ui/presenter.ts` (reverted hardcoded "en-US" to `undefined` for i18n compliance)

target.title: fix(ui): sync message delete to session transcript backend
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Clicking the delete button on a message group in the Control UI only hid the message visually (localStorage) but left the backend .jsonl transcript untouched. The AI could still see the "deleted" content on the next turn.
- **Why it matters:** Users believe a message has been permanently removed, but the AI continues to respond based on that content -- a data inconsistency that creates both a confusing UX and a potential privacy risk.
- **What changed:** Added `sessions.truncate` RPC method, wired it through the protocol layer, gateway handler, and UI so that deleting a message actually truncates the backend transcript from that seq onward.
- **What did NOT change:** Existing `sessions.compact`, `sessions.reset`, or any other session mutation paths are untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History

- **Root cause:** The `onDelete` handler in the chat view only added the message group key to a localStorage-backed `DeletedMessages` set -- it never called any backend RPC to modify the .jsonl transcript.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No integration test verified that deleting a message in the UI actually affected the transcript file.
- **Prior context:** The `sessions.compact` and `sessions.reset` RPC methods already existed with full protocol/handler/UI wiring; `truncate` was simply never added.
- **Why this regressed now:** The feature was never implemented; localStorage-only deletion was the original (incomplete) approach.

## Changes

### Backend (`src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts`)

Three-phase truncate with full safety:

1. **Phase 1** -- Read-only scan under `acquireSessionWriteLock` to locate the cut point. ENOENT-guarded. Does NOT abort active runs yet.
2. **Phase 2** -- Only if a valid cut exists: `cleanupSessionBeforeMutation` aborts active runs with `reason: "session-delete"`. If cleanup fails, the handler returns an error and the transcript is left untouched.
3. **Phase 3c** -- Re-acquire write lock, re-scan for seq (guards against concurrent `sessions.compact`), write-then-rename: writes truncated content to `.tmp.*`, renames original to `.bak.*`, renames temp to original. Preserves original file mode (`statSync(filePath).mode & 0o777`).

Additional safety:
- `rejectWebchatSessionMutation` gate blocks webchat clients (matching `sessions.patch`/`sessions.delete`)
- `reason: "truncate"` in `emitSessionsChanged` (not "compact")
- Token counts cleared on the session store entry after truncation

### Protocol layer

- `SessionsTruncateParamsSchema` with `key` (NonEmptyString) and `seq` (Integer, minimum: 1)
- AJV validator (`validateSessionsTruncateParams`) and type export
- Registered in `ProtocolSchemas`, `server-methods-list.ts`, and `method-scopes.ts` (operator.write)

### UI (`ui/src/ui/app-render.ts` + `ui/src/ui/views/chat.ts`)

- `onTruncateHistory(seq, onFail)` returns `boolean`: `false` when disconnected (caller skips optimistic hide), `true` when RPC dispatched
- `onFail` callback: called on RPC error OR `truncated: false`; caller's `deleted.restore(item.key)` reverses the optimistic hide
- Session-scoped: captures `state.sessionKey` at dispatch time; ignores `.then`/`.catch` if user navigated away
- Run-ID aware: captures `state.chatRunId` at dispatch; if a new run started during RPC flight, still reloads but does not clear the new run's state
- Only clears `chatMessages`/`chatStream`/`chatRunId` when `result.truncated === true`
- `.catch` does NOT clear chat history (only sets `lastError`)
- Search mode: delete disabled entirely when search is active (merged groups could silently drop non-adjacent history)
- When `__openclaw.seq` metadata is absent (e.g. live-streaming messages before history reload), `onDelete` is a no-op -- the user can retry after the chat reloads

### Swift models

- `SessionsTruncateParams` added to both `apps/macos` and `apps/shared` GatewayModels.swift

## Review Issues Addressed

All P1 and P2 issues from Greptile, Copilot, and Codex reviews have been addressed:

| # | Severity | Issue | Resolution |
|---|----------|-------|------------|
| 1 | P1 | Transcript loss if writeFileSync fails after rename | Write-then-rename: .tmp.* then .bak.* then rename |
| 2 | P1 | Abort active runs before truncating | Phase 2 cleanupSessionBeforeMutation after Phase 1 confirms cut |
| 3 | P1 | Block webchat clients from truncating | rejectWebchatSessionMutation gate |
| 4 | P1 | Preserve transcript file mode | statSync mode passed to writeFileSync |
| 5 | P1 | Avoid local-only delete fallback when seq missing | onDelete is no-op when seq absent |
| 6 | P1 | Compute seq from clicked msg not group head | Delete disabled in search mode |
| 7 | P1 | Serialize truncation with concurrent appends | acquireSessionWriteLock in Phase 1 and Phase 3c |
| 8 | P1 | Avoid returning failure after truncation committed | Cleanup before file write |
| 9 | P1 | Propagate cleanup failures | Cleanup error returns before file modification |
| 10 | P2 | Silent failure when disconnected | Returns false; caller skips optimistic hide |
| 11 | P2 | reason: "compact" for truncate event | Changed to "truncate" |
| 12 | P2 | Defer local delete until truncate confirmed | Boolean return controls optimistic hide |
| 13 | P2 | Avoid aborting on no-op | Phase 1 confirms cut before Phase 2 abort |
| 14 | P2 | Scope callback to initiating session | Session key captured at dispatch |
| 15 | P2 | Don't clear newer run state | Run-ID guard |
| 16 | P2 | Recompute cutpoint under final lock | Phase 3c re-scans under lock |
| 17 | P2 | Don't gate refresh on unchanged run ID | loadChatHistory called regardless |
| 18 | P2 | Preserve run state on no-op | Only clears when truncated: true |
| 19 | P2 | Restore optimistic hide on no-op/error | onFail() before session guard |
| 20 | P2 | Re-check before aborting | Phase 1 before Phase 2 |
| 21 | P2 | Reload even if newer run starts | loadChatHistory in new-run branch |
| 22 | P2 | Handle ENOENT in phase-1 scan | ENOENT catch with fallback |
| 23 | P2 | Restore delete when off-session | onFail() always called first |
| 24 | P2 | Preserve chat history on RPC fail | .catch only sets lastError |
| 25 | P2 | Comment refers to fromSeq | Updated to p.seq |

## Regression Test Plan

- All 30 method-scope classification tests pass
- All 37 protocol tests pass
- All 38 gateway sessions integration tests pass
- `pnpm build` passes cleanly
- `pnpm format` clean

target.title: fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `event.messages` was documented as a generic user-reply channel, but only `/new` and `/reset` actually drained those messages.
- Why it matters: `message:received` hooks could push reply text that was silently dropped, while other lifecycle hooks looked supported even though they had no safe reply target.
- What changed: added a shared internal hook reply-drain helper, kept reset/new command behavior, and now deliver `message:received` hook replies when a routable reply target exists.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not make bootstrap, preprocessed/transcribed, sent, or other lifecycle-only hooks auto-reply.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39688
- Related #40185

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Internal hook replies pushed to `event.messages` are now delivered on `message:received` when OpenClaw has a reply-capable routing target.
- Docs now explicitly limit `event.messages` auto-delivery to reply-capable surfaces instead of implying it works for all hooks.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): internal hooks / inbound reply routing
- Relevant config (redacted): default hook + routeReply test harnesses

### Steps

1. Register an internal hook that pushes text into `event.messages`.
2. Trigger it from `/new` or `message:received`.
3. Verify the reply is routed only when a safe reply target exists.

### Expected

- Reply-capable surfaces route the accumulated hook messages.
- Lifecycle-only surfaces remain non-replying unless they gain an explicit delivery contract later.

### Actual

- Before this fix, `message:received` hook replies were silently dropped.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: `commands-core` still routes reset/new hook replies; `dispatch-from-config` now routes `message:received` hook replies on routable surfaces and skips non-routable ones.
- Edge cases checked: non-routable surfaces do not auto-send hook replies; docs/comments now match that contract.
- What you did **not** verify: live messaging-provider delivery outside the existing routeReply test harnesses.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert `fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces`
- Files/config to restore: `src/auto-reply/reply/internal-hook-replies.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-core.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts`, `docs/automation/hooks.md`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate or unexpected hook replies on inbound turns, or `message:received` hook replies not routing when they should.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `message:received` hook replies use the same route-reply path as other provider-routed responses, so a bad target selection would misroute the hook message.
  - Mitigation: the helper uses existing inbound routing fields (`OriginatingTo`, `To`, channel normalization) and adds explicit tests for routable and non-routable surfaces.
- Risk: this branch also carries a whitespace-only `CONTRIBUTING.md` formatter cleanup required for `pnpm check`.
  - Mitigation: it is isolated in its own commit and does not affect runtime behavior.

commands.author: jason-allen-oneal

target.title: fix(session-write-lock): enable reclaim of locks held past holder's own maxHoldMs
target.body:
## Summary

The session write lock's reclaim mechanism treated `"hold-exceeded"` (past the holder's own `maxHoldMs`) as report-only, meaning a contending process could never remove a lock that was overdue by the holder's own declared contract. Combined with the fact that the in-process watchdog (`setInterval`) cannot fire when the holder's event loop is wedged, this caused session locks to pin indefinitely — producing `session file locked (timeout 60000ms)` for every concurrent writer until the gateway was restarted.

The fix removes `"hold-exceeded"` from `REPORT_ONLY_STALE_LOCK_REASONS`. A holder past its **own recorded `maxHoldMs`** is overdue by its own contract and is now reclaimable by a contender. The conservative `"too-old"` (global `staleMs`) stays report-only — a live holder merely past the global heuristic but within its own `maxHoldMs` is still respected.

### Compatibility

- Operators who configured `session.writeLock.maxHoldMs` or `OPENCLAW_SESSION_WRITE_LOCK_MAX_HOLD_MS` will see the value preserved in the lock file. The change only affects what happens when a lock exceeds that value: contenders can now reclaim it.
- The `remove-if-unchanged` recovery still skips a lock whose file changed (e.g. a concurrent release), so only a genuinely stalled holder loses the lock.
- The `cleanStaleLockFiles` doctor sweep does not pass `respectMaxHold` and is unchanged.

## Changes

- `src/agents/session-write-lock.ts` — drop `"hold-exceeded"` from `REPORT_ONLY_STALE_LOCK_REASONS` (one element + comment).
- `src/agents/session-write-lock.test.ts` — add a regression test: a live OpenClaw-owned lock past its own `maxHoldMs` (but within `staleMs`) is now reclaimed by `acquireSessionWriteLock`, and a live lock merely past global `staleMs` (within its own `maxHoldMs`) is still only reported, not removed.

## Reproduction

Issue #87483: a session write-lock held by a **live** gateway process persisted for 8+ hours past `maxHoldMs`/`staleMs`; the watchdog never reclaimed it, so later requests failed with `session file locked (timeout 60000ms): pid=…`. The reporter's holder process was alive and busy (83% CPU) while holding the lock despite the lock recording `maxHoldMs: 1020000` (17 min).

### Before fix

1. A long-running gateway holds a session write-lock and gets stuck in a synchronous CPU loop (event loop blocked).
2. The holder's in-process watchdog can't fire, so the lock is never self-released past `maxHoldMs`.
3. Another request tries to write the same session; it sees the lock past the holder's `maxHoldMs` but cannot reclaim it because `shouldRemoveContendedLockFile` treats `"hold-exceeded"` as report-only (`REPORT_ONLY_STALE_LOCK_REASONS = {"too-old", "hold-exceeded"}`).
4. The contender fails with a stale/`session file locked` error; the lock persists until the gateway restarts or the file is deleted manually.

### After fix

The contender reclaims the lock (the stalled holder's overdue lock is removed-if-unchanged) and proceeds.

## Real behavior proof

Deterministic reproduction added as a regression test in `src/agents/session-write-lock.test.ts`:

```text
reclaims a live OpenClaw-owned lock that exceeded its own maxHoldMs
does NOT reclaim a live OpenClaw-owned lock merely past global staleMs but within its own maxHoldMs
```

Run after the patch:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-write-lock.test.ts
  session-write-lock.test.ts   Tests  56 passed (56)
```

The new case fails on current `main` exactly as the issue describes — `SessionWriteLockStaleError: session file lock stale (hold-exceeded): … alive=true` — and passes after dropping `"hold-exceeded"` from the report-only set, while all existing cases (including the conservative "report live too-old locks without removing them") stay green.

### Observed result (overdue live holder reclaimed; conservative live holder respected)

Runtime verification (tsx reproduction of the reclaim path):

```text
=== Test 1: Reclaim a live OpenClaw-owned lock past its own maxHoldMs ===
  Lock reclaimed: createdAt is 0ms old (< 5000ms)
  PASS

=== Test 2: Do NOT reclaim a live lock within its own maxHoldMs ===
  Correctly rejected: session file lock stale (too-old): pid=... alive=true ageMs=30001
  PASS
```

### Lock payload before/after

Before:
```json
{"pid": 1302, "createdAt": "…", "maxHoldMs": 300000}
```

After:
```json
{"pid": 1302, "createdAt": "…", "maxHoldMs": 300000}
```

The `maxHoldMs` value itself is unchanged — only the reclaim behavior when that deadline is exceeded changes. A contender that previously saw an unrecoverable stale lock now successfully reclaims and re-acquires.

## Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: reclaiming a live holder's lock could race with a holder still writing the transcript.
  **Mitigation**: no new race is introduced. The target case is a *wedged* holder whose event loop is blocked — it cannot be writing the transcript (transcript writes are async and need the event loop). A holder that is still making progress already releases at `maxHoldMs` by its own in-process watchdog, so `maxHoldMs` was already the hard hold limit before this change; the cross-process reclaim only restores that limit for the wedged case where the watchdog cannot fire. `remove-if-unchanged` additionally skips a lock whose file changed.
- **Risk**: behavior change in session-state concurrency.
  **Mitigation**: the change narrows report-only to the global `staleMs` heuristic; it does not touch thresholds, the watchdog, or the doctor sweep, and the conservative live-`too-old` behavior is preserved (covered by an existing test).

target.title: ACP: add gateway-owned node-backed runtime
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw did not yet have a complete **node-backed ACP runtime** where the gateway owns the durable state and a paired node acts only as the execution worker.
- Why it matters: without that split, restart/replay/reconnect behavior is too dependent on the live process, which makes lease fencing, terminal resolution, and outward delivery recovery harder to reason about and prove.
- What this PR adds:
  - a durable ACP gateway store for sessions, runs, accepted worker events, leases, terminal state, replay targets, and projector checkpoints
  - gateway ingestion of ACP worker events coming back from paired nodes
  - a real node-host ACP worker bridge and gateway/node command plumbing
  - a durable ACP reply projection/replay path so restart recovery can resume from gateway state instead of only from the live iterator
  - startup recovery for incomplete ACP node runs
  - restart/reconnect/cancel/close race hardening with focused restart/replay test coverage
  - architecture / protocol / verification docs for the new backend
- Landing-only follow-up: after rebasing onto current `main`, this PR also includes a few **test-only** isolation fixes in Feishu / Google / Google Chat extension tests that were needed to make the landing branch green. Those are not ACP runtime behavior changes.

## What changed by area

### 1) Durable ACP gateway state

New ACP store/state plumbing under `src/acp/store/**`:

- persists ACP sessions and runs
- persists accepted worker events
- tracks leases / lease epochs
- resolves canonical terminal state on the gateway
- stores replay targets and projector checkpoints separately from the runtime cursor

This is the foundation that lets the gateway stay authoritative even when execution happens on a paired node.

### 2) Gateway ↔ node ACP worker path

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/**`
- `src/infra/node-commands.ts`
- `src/node-host/**`

This adds the node-backed ACP execution path:

- gateway can start/control ACP work on a paired node
- node-host can run the ACP worker role
- worker events stream back to the gateway over the existing node transport
- gateway remains the source of truth for run state and terminal resolution

### 3) Durable reply projection and replay

Main implementation areas:

- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`

This PR moves ACP outward delivery onto a durable replayable path:

- reply targets are persisted per run
- projector checkpoints are advanced only after successful delivery
- restart recovery can replay the unfinished suffix from durable state
- reconnect and duplicate accepted events do not re-deliver already completed outward effects

### 4) Startup recovery / reconnect handling

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
- `src/acp/runtime/acp-node.ts`

This adds the gateway-side recovery flow needed to:

- resume incomplete projection after restart
- keep reconnect handling deterministic
- prevent stale-worker wins
- preserve canonical terminal behavior across restart/replay windows

### 5) Test harnesses and regression coverage

Main test additions/expansions:

- `src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts`
- `src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts`
- `src/acp/test-harness/restart-harness.ts`

These cover the core failure windows this backend needs to survive:

- restart after accepted events but before replay checkpoint advancement
- restart after terminal persistence but before final outward convergence
- reconnect replay of already accepted events
- cancel / complete / close race handling
- restart recovery with a real memory cut instead of reusing in-process objects

### 6) Docs included in this PR

Main docs:

- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-verification.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-runtime-program.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-summary.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-final-judge.md`

These are included because this was developed as a staged architecture / verification effort and the final accepted behavior is documented there.

## Explicit scope boundaries / v1 limitations

This PR does **not**:

- turn nodes into mini-gateways
- add automatic cross-node continuation for an in-flight run
- claim durable resumed tool-message edit support after restart

Current conservative post-restart tool-update behavior remains:

- `append_only_after_restart`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: ACP node runtime architecture / protocol / verification docs under `docs/experiments/plans/`
- Related: landing-only extension test isolation fixes after rebase onto current `main`

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenClaw now has a first-class ACP node-backed runtime path where the gateway owns durable ACP truth and paired nodes execute the worker role.
- Gateway restart/recovery can resume incomplete ACP outward projection from durable state.
- Duplicate accepted worker-event replay on reconnect is hardened so it does not duplicate outward delivery.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally follow the documented conservative v1 rule: `append_only_after_restart`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? Yes

### Security / trust-boundary notes

- Paired nodes can now advertise ACP worker capability and receive ACP runtime commands over the existing gateway↔node trust boundary.
- Gateway↔node websocket traffic now carries ACP worker control / event flows in addition to existing node commands.
- Gateway now persists ACP runtime state needed for durable replay/recovery.
- The design mitigates stale-worker / duplicate-delivery risks via lease epochs, canonical terminal resolution, durable replay checkpoints, and reconnect/replay handling on the gateway.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (landing worktree on vm1) + GitHub Actions CI
- Runtime/container: Node / pnpm; Bun build path also checked
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): ACP gateway↔node runtime path
- Relevant config (redacted): ACP runtime restart/replay harnesses and paired-node fixtures

### Verified locally

- ACP targeted verification gate:
  - `pnpm test -- src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts src/acp/store/store.test.ts src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts src/acp/store/gateway-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.acp.test.ts`
- TypeScript guardrail:
  - `pnpm tsgo`
- Landing-only extension test fixes:
  - `pnpm test:extensions`

### CI / build notes

- `pnpm build` still fails only with the pre-existing Bun wrapper issue:
  - `Cannot find module './cjs/index.cjs' from ''`
- That build failure was already present and is not introduced by the ACP runtime changes in this PR.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the ACP-focused local gate listed above.
- Verified `pnpm tsgo` passes.
- Verified the rebased landing branch’s extension test-isolation fixes with local `pnpm test:extensions`.
- Verified the PR now includes the architecture / protocol / verification / final-judge docs for this backend.

What I did **not** verify:

- I did **not** get a clean `pnpm build` because of the pre-existing Bun wrapper failure noted above.
- I did **not** run a fresh manual end-to-end Discord/real-node interactive repro outside the checked-in restart/replay and node-host harnesses.

## Review Guide

If you want the shortest path through this PR, review in this order:

1. **Core architecture / intent**
   - `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
   - `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
2. **Gateway-owned durable state**
   - `src/acp/store/**`
3. **Node execution bridge**
   - `src/node-host/**`
   - `src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.ts`
   - `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
4. **Durable outward replay path**
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`
5. **Recovery and proofs**
   - `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
   - `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
6. **Ignore as landing-only test fixes unless you are checking CI fallout**
   - `extensions/feishu/**`
   - `extensions/google/**`
   - `extensions/googlechat/**`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No explicit migration required
- Migration needed? No

Notes:

- This adds a new ACP runtime/backend path; it does not force existing deployments to switch to it immediately.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally remain append-only in v1.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR or avoid selecting/using the `acp-node` runtime path until fixed.
- Files/config to restore: ACP runtime/store/gateway/node-host changes under `src/acp/**`, `src/node-host/**`, `src/gateway/**`, plus the accompanying docs if reverting wholesale.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate final delivery after restart, stale worker winning after reconnect, projector checkpoint drift, or replay targeting the wrong route.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: restart/replay correctness is subtle in any durable projection system.
  - Mitigation: this PR includes restart harnesses, startup recovery tests, reconnect/replay ordering tests, and explicit protocol/verification docs.
- Risk: the PR is large and spans multiple ACP runtime layers.
  - Mitigation: the review guide above splits it into durable store, node bridge, replay path, recovery path, and landing-only CI fixes.
- Risk: paired-node ACP execution expands the gateway↔node command surface.
  - Mitigation: the gateway remains authoritative for leases, terminal resolution, checkpoints, and replay instead of trusting node-local completion.

---

## AI Checklist

- [x] **AI-assisted** — agent-harness / Codex driven implementation, review, and landing follow-ups
- [x] **Testing degree** — focused local verification plus CI
- [x] **Understanding confirmed** — this PR adds the gateway-owned ACP node-backed runtime and includes a few landing-only extension test fixes after rebase
- [ ] Prompts/session logs — available on request

target.title: fix: serialize outbound deliveries per channel+recipient
target.body:
## Summary

When multiple sessions (main + sub-agent) send messages to the same recipient concurrently, replies can arrive out of order because each session's delivery call races independently on the channel's HTTP endpoint. Users see jumbled message sequences — especially noticeable on Telegram and Discord where ordering matters for readability.

**Reproduction:** Run a main agent and sub-agent that both reply to the same user within a short window. Without this fix, the second session's message frequently lands before the first's, depending on network timing.

**Fix:** Introduces a `DeliverySerializer` that chains concurrent deliveries to the same `channel:accountId:recipient` key in FIFO order while keeping different recipients fully concurrent. The serializer is in-memory only (runtime ordering, not persistence) and auto-cleans keys when queues drain. The existing write-ahead delivery queue for crash recovery is unaffected.

A follow-up commit wraps `fn()` in `Promise.resolve().then()` so a synchronous throw is converted to a rejection rather than hanging the result promise forever (addresses review feedback).

Includes tests for ordering, concurrency across targets, error propagation, sync-throw handling, and queue cleanup.

Rebased to 2026.3.13.

---

## Real behavior proof — Telegram, live session (2026-05-22)

This race was observed live in production during this PR's own review session on **Telegram** (not Signal).

**What happened:** During a single agent turn handling `pr view 44143`, two outbound messages were sent in sequence to the same Telegram recipient (`telegram:6194445555`):
1. A long ClawSweeper analysis dump (~4KB)
2. A summary message referencing and synthesizing that analysis

**Observed:** The summary (message 2) arrived visibly before the analysis (message 1) in the Telegram chat. The recipient had to read the synthesis before the content it was synthesizing — the exact inversion this PR fixes.

**Context:** Telegram direct message, same `channel:accountId:to` key, single agent session, two sends within the same turn. No sub-agent involved — the race can happen within a single session turn when one send is large enough to lose the HTTP timing race against a smaller follow-up send.

**Telegram message IDs at time of observation:** user message `5519` ("we're experiencing that right now here in Telegram. Look at your last 3 messages"), preceded by the two inverted agent messages. Timestamp: 2026-05-22 ~15:00 EDT.

This confirms the bug is not Signal-specific and reproduces on Telegram in normal production usage without any artificial test setup.

target.title: fix(mattermost): resolve private-channel chat_type from the real channel kind, not the lossy target prefix
target.body:
Fixes #95646
Related: #95645

## What Problem This Solves

The `chat_type` OpenClaw reports for a Mattermost **private channel** is non-deterministic across code paths: it is `group` when derived from the channel's real type (the authoritative inbound path), but `channel` when derived from the delivery target string (the lossy outbound/heartbeat path). Mattermost addresses private channels as `channel:<id>` — the same prefix as a public channel — so the lossy path can't tell them apart. The same conversation can end up keyed under two different session namespaces (`group:<id>:thread:<root>` from inbound, `channel:<id>:thread:<root>` from a delivery-side session), causing threaded/scheduled deliveries bound to one namespace to fail to match the other.

## Why This Change Was Made

OpenClaw already has a per-plugin extension point for exactly this — `messaging.inferTargetChatType({ to })` — implemented by Telegram, Discord, Signal, iMessage, and ClickClack, but not Mattermost. Two problems needed fixing together:

1. **Mattermost never implemented the hook.** The real channel type (`D`/`O`/`P`/`G`) is only known on the inbound path, resolved asynchronously via `resolveChannelInfo` (in `monitor-resources.ts`) against the Mattermost API. `inferTargetChatType` is a synchronous, best-effort hook (`(params: { to: string }) => ChatType | undefined`) — it can't make a network call. New `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/channel-kind-store.ts` adds a tiny process-wide cache (`rememberMattermostChannelKind` / `peekMattermostChannelKind`) that `resolveChannelInfo` now populates as a side effect of its existing async resolution, whenever it has a fresh answer. The new `inferTargetChatType` hook on Mattermost's `channel.ts` reads from this cache synchronously — correct once the channel has been seen at least once (the common case), falling back to `undefined` (today's lossy default) otherwise, never worse than before.
2. **The generic dispatchers short-circuited before consulting any plugin hook.** Both `src/infra/outbound/targets.ts` (`inferChatTypeFromTarget`) and `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts` (`inferDeliveryTargetChatType`) returned the hardcoded `"channel"` for any `channel:`/`thread:`-prefixed target *before* checking `plugin.messaging.inferTargetChatType`, so even a correctly implemented hook would never be reached for that shape. Both now consult the plugin hook first and only fall back to `"channel"` when the hook has no answer.

## User Impact

Mattermost private-channel conversations get a consistent, correct `group` chat type instead of being split across two session namespaces depending on whether the path was inbound or outbound/heartbeat delivery. Public channels (`O`) are unaffected — they correctly remain `channel`. Other channels are unaffected: the generic dispatcher change is purely additive (consult the hook first, same fallback as before when the hook returns nothing), and no other current plugin's `inferTargetChatType` implementation returns a different answer for `channel:`/`thread:`-shaped targets than the existing default.

## Evidence

Behavior addressed: Mattermost private channels (`P`) and true multi-user groups (`G`) report `chat_type: "channel"` on the outbound/heartbeat/delivery path instead of the authoritative `"group"`, because (a) Mattermost has no `inferTargetChatType` hook and (b) the generic dispatcher never reaches a plugin hook for `channel:`-prefixed targets regardless.

Real environment tested: macOS arm64 (M4), Darwin 25.5.0, Node v25.9.0, branch `fix/mattermost-private-channel-chat-type`, commit `3cb04b1c4e`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts src/infra/outbound/targets-loaded.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/channel.ts extensions/mattermost/src/channel.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/channel-kind-store.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-resources.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-resources.test.ts src/infra/outbound/targets.ts src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:extensions && pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
```

Evidence after fix:
```
extensions/mattermost: 41 files, 512 passed (512)
targets.test.ts + targets-loaded.test.ts: 84 passed (84)
subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts: 105 passed (105)
oxlint: exit 0, no findings
tsgo:core / tsgo:extensions / tsgo:core:test / tsgo:extensions:test: exit 0
```

Observed result after fix: added 7 new regression tests covering: (1) `resolveChannelInfo` remembers a private channel's kind as `"group"` in the new store, (2) Mattermost's `inferTargetChatType` returns the remembered kind for a `channel:` target and `undefined` when never seen, (3) `inferChatTypeFromTarget` (via `resolveHeartbeatDeliveryTargetWithSessionRoute`) now returns the plugin's override instead of the hardcoded `"channel"` for a `channel:` target, with a paired test confirming the `"channel"` fallback still applies when the plugin has no hook, (4) the same two cases for `inferDeliveryTargetChatType` in `subagent-announce-delivery.ts` (exposed via the existing `testing` namespace for direct unit coverage, since its only current caller — `isDirectMessageDeliveryTarget` — only checks for `"direct"` and doesn't otherwise observe the group/channel distinction). Confirmed all new tests fail against the pre-fix code (by reverting each source file individually while keeping its test) and pass after restoring the fix.

What was not tested: did not exercise this against a live Mattermost server (no credentials/test workspace in this environment) — this is a classifier/cache-wiring unit fix verified against the exact derivation mismatch described in the issue, not a live end-to-end private-channel round trip. The `inferDeliveryTargetChatType` fix in `subagent-announce-delivery.ts` currently has no externally observable effect through its only call site (`isDirectMessageDeliveryTarget` only distinguishes direct vs. non-direct) — it's included for derivation consistency with the documented bug pattern and to be correct for any future caller that does care about the group/channel distinction.

target.title: Add Eden AI provider plugin
target.body:
## Summary

Add Eden AI as a bundled provider plugin.

- Problem: OpenClaw users who want to route through Eden AI's meta-gateway (349 models, 21 underlying vendors) currently have to write a manual `models.providers.edenai` config and pick a base URL by hand. There is no bundled plugin, no `--auth-choice`, no docs page.
- Why it matters: Eden AI is positioned alongside OpenRouter and Vercel AI Gateway as one of the few OpenAI-compatible meta-gateways. Users already comfortable with `openclaw onboard --auth-choice openrouter-api-key` expect the same flow for Eden AI.
- What changed: a new bundled plugin at `extensions/edenai/`, mirroring `extensions/openrouter/` minus the speech / image / video / media capabilities. Text inference only. Includes onboarding, static eight-model catalog, live `/v3/models` discovery via the OpenAI-compatible transport, `EDENAI_API_KEY` auth, `--edenai-api-key` CLI flag, `edenai-api-key` wizard choice, aliases (`eden`, `eden-ai`, `eden_ai`), Mintlify provider page, `.env.example` row, and a labeler entry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no edits to `src/**`, no changes to `CHANGELOG.md`, no other extension directory touched, no embeddings or image/audio/speech capabilities (those are follow-up PRs).

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: bundled Eden AI provider plugin so `openclaw onboard --auth-choice edenai-api-key` and `openclaw chat --provider edenai` work out of the box.
- Real environment tested: macOS / Linux dev shell, OpenClaw built from this branch (`pnpm build` clean).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  export EDENAI_API_KEY=...           # real key from app.edenai.run/admin/account/settings
  pnpm openclaw onboard --non-interactive \
      --auth-choice edenai-api-key \
      --edenai-api-key "$EDENAI_API_KEY"
  pnpm openclaw models list --provider edenai | head
  pnpm openclaw chat --provider edenai --model edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 -q "Reply with exactly OK."
  OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 EDENAI_API_KEY="$EDENAI_API_KEY" \
      pnpm test extensions/edenai/edenai.live.test.ts
  ```
- Evidence after fix: see the screenshot/terminal capture attached to the PR. Live `chat/completions` returns `OK`, the live `/v3/models` catalog (500+ rows) is warmed by `prepareDynamicModel` and feeds dynamic resolution with real per-model metadata, and the gated live vitest passes.
- Observed result after fix: provider is selectable from the wizard; `openclaw chat` returns a real assistant reply from multiple underlying vendors via Eden AI; `openclaw models list --provider edenai` shows the curated offline catalog while live discovery powers the runtime model resolution path.
- What was not tested: image generation, audio transcription, TTS, and embeddings (intentionally out of scope for this PR; see Out of scope below). Codex review was not run locally because the contributor does not have Codex access.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A (feature add, not a bug fix).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/edenai/onboard.test.ts`, `extensions/edenai/provider-catalog.test.ts`, `extensions/edenai/edenai.live.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: provider registers under id `edenai` with `EDENAI_API_KEY` auth, the static catalog ships exactly eight models with Eden AI's hyphen Anthropic id form, `normalizeEdenaiBaseUrl` canonicalises `/v3` and legacy `/v2` paths, onboard preserves the alias and primary model, and dynamic model resolution hits Eden AI's live `/v3/chat/completions` end to end (gated).
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: catalog drift between Eden AI's published model ids and the static fallback is the most likely silent failure; the eight-entry test asserts the hyphen ids verbatim. Base-URL normalisation is the next-most-likely regression as Eden AI's API moves from `/v2` to `/v3`.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New auth choice in the onboarding wizard: "Eden AI API key" (group "Eden AI").
- New CLI flag `--edenai-api-key <key>` and direct env var `EDENAI_API_KEY`.
- New model-ref shape `edenai/<vendor>/<model>` (for example `edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`).
- Anthropic models on Eden AI use the hyphen id form (`claude-opus-4-6`), not the dot form (`claude-opus-4.6`). The bundled catalog uses the hyphen form Eden AI returns; Eden AI rejects the dot form with HTTP 400. Manual `models.providers.edenai.models[].id` entries should follow Eden AI's form, not the upstream vendor's.
- Eden AI wraps `response_format: {"type": "json_object"}` output in a ` ```json ... ``` ` code fence. OpenClaw's parser does not require strict `JSON.parse` on assistant text, so this is cosmetic; consumers that need raw JSON should strip the fence.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[user]
   |
   v
manual models.providers.edenai = { baseUrl, apiKey, api: "openai-completions", models: [...] }

After:
[user] -> openclaw onboard --auth-choice edenai-api-key
              |
              v
        bundled plugin (extensions/edenai)
              |
              v
        OpenAI-compatible /v3/chat/completions -> 21 underlying vendors
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - reuses the existing `EDENAI_API_KEY` env var convention via the shared `createProviderApiKeyAuthMethod` helper.
- New/changed network calls? Yes - outbound `GET https://api.edenai.run/v3/models` (during live catalog discovery) and the standard `POST /v3/chat/completions` for inference. Both go to a single host suffix (`api.edenai.run`).
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No - users explicitly opt in by setting their own key.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the network calls share the same SSRF guard, timeout, and error handling that every other OpenAI-compatible provider plugin uses (via `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`). Eden AI's endpoint is publicly documented and the only outbound host is `api.edenai.run`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux x86_64 (also exercised on macOS arm64).
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.0.
- Model/provider: `edenai` (default model `edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`).
- Integration/channel (if any): n/a (provider plugin only).
- Relevant config (redacted): `EDENAI_API_KEY=ek_...` in `~/.openclaw/.env`.

### Steps

1. `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test extensions/edenai/`
2. `pnpm openclaw onboard --non-interactive --auth-choice edenai-api-key --edenai-api-key "$EDENAI_API_KEY"`
3. `pnpm openclaw models list --provider edenai`
4. `pnpm openclaw chat --provider edenai --model edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 -q "Reply with exactly OK."`

### Expected

- `pnpm check` clean (typecheck, oxlint, contract checks).
- `pnpm test extensions/edenai` shows 6 unit tests passing (live test skipped when `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST` is unset).
- Step 2 prints the configured provider and selected default model.
- Step 3 prints the eight bundled static entries plus the full live catalog when the key resolves.
- Step 4 returns `OK` (or `OK.`/`OK!`).

### Actual

- `pnpm check` clean (lint, typecheck, runtime sidecar guard, dependency pin guard, runtime import cycles - all green).
- `pnpm test:extension edenai` -> 2 test files, 6 tests passing in \~4 s; live test correctly skipped.
- Live chat with `edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` returns `OK.` - see attached terminal capture.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (new vitest files; no prior coverage to compare against).
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording (terminal capture of `openclaw chat --provider edenai`, attached as a PR comment because GitHub does not let contributors inline-attach artifacts in the description).
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI):

- Verified scenarios: `openclaw onboard --auth-choice edenai-api-key` flow with a real key; `models list --provider edenai` against the live catalog; `chat` with `edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` reaching Eden AI and getting an assistant reply.
- Edge cases checked: missing key falls back to the static eight-model catalog (no network); `EDENAI_API_KEY=invalid` surfaces the upstream HTTP 401 cleanly through OpenClaw's standard provider error path.
- What you did **not** verify: image-generation, audio, TTS, and embeddings capabilities (deliberately out of scope; see "Out of scope" in the diagram block).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes - additive only.
- Config/env changes? Yes - new optional env var `EDENAI_API_KEY` and CLI flag `--edenai-api-key`; both unset by default and have no effect on existing setups.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: n/a.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Eden AI's live `/v3/models` catalog includes ids that are not yet in the static fallback list. The static list (eight curated entries) only fires when `EDENAI_API_KEY` is unset.
  - Mitigation: `resolveDynamicModel` accepts any `edenai/<vendor>/<model>` id and synthesises a `ProviderRuntimeModel` with sensible defaults if the live catalog hasn't been fetched yet. Behavior matches OpenRouter's `resolveDynamicModel` path.
- Risk: future Eden AI gateway updates could move endpoints again (the plugin already handles the `/v2` -> `/v3` rename via `normalizeEdenaiBaseUrl`).
  - Mitigation: tests assert the canonical and known-legacy base URLs both normalise to `/v3`. Adding a new legacy variant is a single-line change.

## Out of scope (follow-ups)

- Embeddings via `registerEmbeddingProvider` (Eden AI exposes `/v3/embeddings`).
- Image generation via `registerImageGenerationProvider` (`/v3/image/generations`).
- Audio transcription via `registerMediaUnderstandingProvider({ capabilities: ["audio"] })` (`/v3/audio/transcriptions`).
- Text-to-speech via `registerSpeechProvider` (`/v3/audio/speech`).
- Reasoning policy (`resolveThinkingProfile`): Eden AI does not currently forward Anthropic's `thinking` budget or OpenAI's reasoning-effort knob in a uniform way. Defer until Eden AI exposes the parameter.

target.title: fix(mattermost): resolve private-channel chat_type from the real channel kind, not the lossy target prefix
target.body:
Fixes #95646
Related: #95645

## What Problem This Solves

The `chat_type` OpenClaw reports for a Mattermost **private channel** is non-deterministic across code paths: it is `group` when derived from the channel's real type (the authoritative inbound path), but `channel` when derived from the delivery target string (the lossy outbound/heartbeat path). Mattermost addresses private channels as `channel:<id>` — the same prefix as a public channel — so the lossy path can't tell them apart. The same conversation can end up keyed under two different session namespaces (`group:<id>:thread:<root>` from inbound, `channel:<id>:thread:<root>` from a delivery-side session), causing threaded/scheduled deliveries bound to one namespace to fail to match the other.

## Why This Change Was Made

OpenClaw already has a per-plugin extension point for exactly this — `messaging.inferTargetChatType({ to })` — implemented by Telegram, Discord, Signal, iMessage, and ClickClack, but not Mattermost. Two problems needed fixing together:

1. **Mattermost never implemented the hook.** The real channel type (`D`/`O`/`P`/`G`) is only known on the inbound path, resolved asynchronously via `resolveChannelInfo` (in `monitor-resources.ts`) against the Mattermost API. `inferTargetChatType` is a synchronous, best-effort hook (`(params: { to: string }) => ChatType | undefined`) — it can't make a network call. New `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/channel-kind-store.ts` adds a tiny process-wide cache (`rememberMattermostChannelKind` / `peekMattermostChannelKind`) that `resolveChannelInfo` now populates as a side effect of its existing async resolution, whenever it has a fresh answer. The new `inferTargetChatType` hook on Mattermost's `channel.ts` reads from this cache synchronously — correct once the channel has been seen at least once (the common case), falling back to `undefined` (today's lossy default) otherwise, never worse than before.
2. **The generic dispatchers short-circuited before consulting any plugin hook.** Both `src/infra/outbound/targets.ts` (`inferChatTypeFromTarget`) and `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts` (`inferDeliveryTargetChatType`) returned the hardcoded `"channel"` for any `channel:`/`thread:`-prefixed target *before* checking `plugin.messaging.inferTargetChatType`, so even a correctly implemented hook would never be reached for that shape. Both now consult the plugin hook first and only fall back to `"channel"` when the hook has no answer.

## User Impact

Mattermost private-channel conversations get a consistent, correct `group` chat type instead of being split across two session namespaces depending on whether the path was inbound or outbound/heartbeat delivery. Public channels (`O`) are unaffected — they correctly remain `channel`. Other channels are unaffected: the generic dispatcher change is purely additive (consult the hook first, same fallback as before when the hook returns nothing), and no other current plugin's `inferTargetChatType` implementation returns a different answer for `channel:`/`thread:`-shaped targets than the existing default.

## Evidence

Behavior addressed: Mattermost private channels (`P`) and true multi-user groups (`G`) report `chat_type: "channel"` on the outbound/heartbeat/delivery path instead of the authoritative `"group"`, because (a) Mattermost has no `inferTargetChatType` hook and (b) the generic dispatcher never reaches a plugin hook for `channel:`-prefixed targets regardless.

Real environment tested: macOS arm64 (M4), Darwin 25.5.0, Node v25.9.0, branch `fix/mattermost-private-channel-chat-type`, commit `3cb04b1c4e`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts src/infra/outbound/targets-loaded.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/channel.ts extensions/mattermost/src/channel.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/channel-kind-store.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-resources.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-resources.test.ts src/infra/outbound/targets.ts src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:extensions && pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
```

Evidence after fix:
```
extensions/mattermost: 41 files, 512 passed (512)
targets.test.ts + targets-loaded.test.ts: 84 passed (84)
subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts: 105 passed (105)
oxlint: exit 0, no findings
tsgo:core / tsgo:extensions / tsgo:core:test / tsgo:extensions:test: exit 0
```

Observed result after fix: added 7 new regression tests covering: (1) `resolveChannelInfo` remembers a private channel's kind as `"group"` in the new store, (2) Mattermost's `inferTargetChatType` returns the remembered kind for a `channel:` target and `undefined` when never seen, (3) `inferChatTypeFromTarget` (via `resolveHeartbeatDeliveryTargetWithSessionRoute`) now returns the plugin's override instead of the hardcoded `"channel"` for a `channel:` target, with a paired test confirming the `"channel"` fallback still applies when the plugin has no hook, (4) the same two cases for `inferDeliveryTargetChatType` in `subagent-announce-delivery.ts` (exposed via the existing `testing` namespace for direct unit coverage, since its only current caller — `isDirectMessageDeliveryTarget` — only checks for `"direct"` and doesn't otherwise observe the group/channel distinction). Confirmed all new tests fail against the pre-fix code (by reverting each source file individually while keeping its test) and pass after restoring the fix.

What was not tested: did not exercise this against a live Mattermost server (no credentials/test workspace in this environment) — this is a classifier/cache-wiring unit fix verified against the exact derivation mismatch described in the issue, not a live end-to-end private-channel round trip. The `inferDeliveryTargetChatType` fix in `subagent-announce-delivery.ts` currently has no externally observable effect through its only call site (`isDirectMessageDeliveryTarget` only distinguishes direct vs. non-direct) — it's included for derivation consistency with the documented bug pattern and to be correct for any future caller that does care about the group/channel distinction.

target.title: feat(claude): add claude-bridge app-server harness extension
target.body:
## Summary

TL;DR:
Inspiration: https://openclaw.ai/blog/openai-models-in-openclaw-done-right; goal: achieve parity for Claude

New feature; issue https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/93793

OpenClaw currently has no first-class harness for Anthropic models. Anthropic users either fall back to a generic provider client (no native tool execution, no extended-thinking awareness, no per-turn rate-limit surfacing) or run a Claude harness out-of-tree with no contract for OpenClaw's plugin-SDK capabilities and features.

The codex harness already ships in-tree with the same shape we want — `extensions/codex/src/app-server/` is a codex-app-server JSON-RPC client over stdio. We have a working Anthropic-side equivalent built and running in production on the author's setup; opening it for maintainer steer before doing more SDK work on top.

Harness: this PR
JSON-RPC server: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge

**What is the intended outcome?**

- Bundled extension `@openclaw/claude` registers `claude-bridge` as a harness option in the model picker.
- At turn-start, the extension spawns an `openclaw-claude-bridge` subprocess and speaks the codex-shaped JSON-RPC protocol over stdio, directly wrapping the latest claude agent SDK. The server (currently published as `@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge`  https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge — fork-preview path; would migrate to `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` if the maintainers republish it) handles Anthropic API I/O, rate-limit surfacing, image-payload sanitization, and plugin-thread-config persistence.
- Bridge-side handles event projection into OpenClaw's notification/accumulator surface, transcript mirroring with idempotency keys, dynamic-tool aggregate output cap, vision-tools de-duplication, and `/claude` slash commands.
- See `extensions/claude/README.md` for the full feature map.

**What is intentionally out of scope?**

- Republishing the server under `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` — needs maintainer decision; the `@zeroaltitude` scope dependency is the current placeholder.
- Migrating to OpenClaw's standard plugin-SDK provider hooks beyond what `extensions/codex/` already uses. The bridge follows codex's pattern verbatim where applicable.
- Anything outside the harness bridge surface.

**What does success look like?** Anthropic users get parity with codex users: native tool dispatch, rate-limit-aware errors, extended-thinking-friendly transcript mirroring, harness-level event projection. No regressions to existing harnesses.

**What should reviewers focus on?**

1. **Scope concern — am I taking too much core?** The branch touches `src/plugins/command-registration.ts`, `src/plugins/current-plugin-metadata-snapshot.ts`, `src/plugins/agent-tool-result-middleware-types.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-native-slash-fast-path.ts`, `src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.ts`, and `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`. Each is small and necessary to wire a harness extension, but the contribution guide says "one thing per PR." Happy to split into prep PRs first if maintainers prefer.
2. **External-plugin vs. bundled-extension** — Per CONTRIBUTING.md "most features should be third-party plugins." Open to packaging this entirely as an external plugin instead of `extensions/claude/`, if that is preferred.
3. **Anthropic-API behavior delegated to a separate server repo** — Most of the actual API logic lives in the server (https://github.com/zeroaltitude/openclaw-plugins/tree/main/openclaw-claude/server -- https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge). Reviewers will probably want to inspect that companion repo before approving.
4. **`claude-bridge` harness id naming** — there's no precedent for "<vendor>-bridge" harness names; renamable. (The reason I did this initially was to ensure that I wasn't representing that this work was by anthropic -- matters more on the server naming than the in-openclaw extension tho)

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Anthropic-model agents previously had no in-tree harness; the bridge unlocks native tool runs against Claude through the same JSON-RPC pattern codex uses.
- **Real environment tested:** Author's openclaw gateway running on Linux (WSL2 / Ubuntu), Node 22.x. Author's agents  are configured with `claude-bridge` as their harness and are in daily active use against `claude-sonnet-4-6` and `claude-opus-4-7`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `pnpm install && pnpm build`
  2. `openclaw gateway restart --deep`
  3. Send a Slack DM to one of the claude-bridge agents
  4. Agent responds with native Anthropic tools used; transcript file contains the projected events.
  5. `/claude status` in the same thread prints shared-client liveness.
- **Evidence after fix (fresh capture, 2026-05-28):** This gateway runs the claude-bridge harness as the default runtime for all Anthropic models (`agents.defaults.models.*.agentRuntime.id = "claude-bridge"`), so the harness is exercised continuously across multiple agents. Captured live:
  1. **Bridge liveness** — shared-client lifecycle today: `claude-bridge: spawned ×5 / initialized ×5 / process exited ×5` (restarts and re-initializes without manual intervention).
  2. **A turn completing with visible tool/progress output** — one bridge turn dispatching consecutive tools, each completing, with per-tool provenance timing from the observability plugin: `ToolSearch → after_tool_call 1.9ms`, `exec → 1.7ms`, `Write → 1.9ms`, `Agent → 1.9ms`.
  3. **Transcript / result event stream** — the run persists a projected event stream to `~/.openclaw/agents/<id>/sessions/<uuid>.jsonl`: parentId-chained `message` events covering the user turn, assistant `tool_use` blocks, and `tool_result` blocks.
  4. **`/claude status`** (live, from a bridge-backed agent):
     ```
     Claude app-server status
     Shared client: running
     Command: openclaw-claude-bridge
     Last stderr: [turn-runner] system event {"subtype":"init"} / {"subtype":"status"} / unhandled SDK event {"type":"rate_limit_event"}
     ```
  *Log-hygiene note:* a few `claude-bridge: runAttempt failed` lines appear in the same logs; they are not bridge defects — one class is a context-overflow on a zero-message scheduled run (auto-compaction correctly skipped), the other is transient model-call failover during an MCP reconnect; both recovered on the next turn. Earlier daily-driver context still applies: Tabitha (claude-bridge) has been in active production use for ~2 weeks.
- **Observed result after fix:** Anthropic turns complete normally; tool calls fire; rate-limit messages are user-actionable; resume across restart works via the `.claude-binding.json` sidecar.
- **What was not tested:**
  - Windows/macOS gateway hosts (only Linux/WSL2 verified).
  - Bun runtime (Node 22 only).
  - Anthropic enterprise/Bedrock/Vertex API surfaces (only public Anthropic API).
  - Concurrent multi-account scenarios beyond 3 agents.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Author runs a single-host gateway. The server is currently published under the author's `@zeroaltitude` npm scope (fork-preview), so reproducing this requires installing the bridge from that scope until maintainers republish.
- **Before evidence:** N/A — no prior in-tree harness for Anthropic to compare against.

## Tests and validation

- Real world use for over a month on a broad diversity of tasks with observed, anecdotal, improvements in coding, token usage and well executed long running tasks
- `pnpm build` ✓ (local)
- `pnpm test extensions/claude` ✓ (local — extension unit tests, including event projector, dynamic-tools middleware port, thread fork policy envelope, transcript mirror dedupe)
- `pnpm test:extensions` not run since last rebase — recommend Testbox/Crabbox run before non-draft review.
- No `CHANGELOG.md` edit per contributor rule; maintainer to add on land.

## Risk checklist

- **Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — new `claude-bridge` harness appears in model picker; new `/claude` slash command surface.
- **Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** Yes — new plugin `configSchema` (see `extensions/claude/openclaw.plugin.json`). Existing agent configs unaffected unless they explicitly opt into `claude-bridge` harness. Doctor contract added (`extensions/claude/doctor-contract-api.ts`).
- **Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** Yes (intentional). The bridge spawns a subprocess that holds the Anthropic API key in-memory and makes network calls to Anthropic. Subprocess lifecycle is owned by the harness, not user shell. The plugin SDK enabler changes (e.g. `command-registration.ts`) are additive and follow existing codex-extension precedent.
- **Highest-risk area:** Server-bridge stdio lifecycle and the few SDK enabler edits in `src/plugins/**`. If the SDK changes accidentally relax a guard for non-claude paths, every harness would inherit the change.
- **How is that risk mitigated?** The SDK changes are narrowly scoped — each follows a codex-extension-shaped precedent. Happy to factor them into a prep PR if reviewers want to land them separately first.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** Maintainer steer on scope: (a) accept as bundled extension as-is, (b) split SDK enablers into a prep PR first, or (c) request packaging as an external plugin.
- **Still waiting on:** Maintainer decision on the above. CI run on this push.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** None yet — opening draft.

---

🤖 **AI-assisted disclosure:** Developed with the assistance of Claude Code (anthropic.com/claude-code). All design decisions, production deployment, and real-behavior verification are human-driven; AI assistance was used for implementation across multiple sessions. Session transcripts available on request.

target.title: fix(agents): cap DSML recovery buffer to prevent unbounded memory growth
target.body:
Fixes #90015

## Summary

The `createDeepSeekDsmlToolCallRecoverer()` accumulates text in a buffer while waiting for a DSML close token. A malicious or buggy provider response could send an extremely large DSML body without ever closing it, causing unbounded memory growth.

Add a 256 KB cap (`MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES`) that discards the current DSML block as plain text when the buffer exceeds the limit. This matches the existing 256 KB caps used for native tool call argument buffers (`MAX_TOOL_CALL_ARGUMENT_BUFFER_BYTES`) and post-tool-call text buffers (`MAX_POST_TOOL_CALL_BUFFER_BYTES`).

## Changed files

- `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts` -- add `MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES` constant with UTF-8 byte-accurate measurement (`Buffer.byteLength`) in `createDeepSeekDsmlToolCallRecoverer()` when no close token is found
- `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts` -- regression tests: oversized DSML block discarded as text (ASCII), and multibyte UTF-8 payload that exceeds byte cap despite fitting in string length

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The DSML recovery buffer in `createDeepSeekDsmlToolCallRecoverer()` grows without bound when a provider sends a DSML open token followed by a large body that never closes. Additionally, the byte-named cap was enforced using `.length` (UTF-16 code units), which undercounts multibyte UTF-8 text, allowing payloads above the intended 256 KB byte limit to bypass the guard.

- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node v22.19, OpenClaw built from patched source at `/tmp/wt-86637`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Step 1. Built from branch and installed deps:

```bash
cd /tmp/wt-86637
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build
```

Step 2. Verified the fix uses byte-accurate measurement in the compiled production bundle:

```bash
node -e "
const chunk = require('fs').readFileSync('dist/openai-transport-stream-DRlicd4V.js', 'utf8');
const capMatch = chunk.match(/MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES\s*=\s*([\d.e]+)/);
console.log('DSML recovery buffer cap:', eval(capMatch[1]) + ' bytes');
const usesBytes = chunk.includes('Buffer.byteLength(buffer, \"utf8\") > MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES');
const usesLength = chunk.includes('buffer.length > MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES');
console.log('Uses Buffer.byteLength:', usesBytes);
console.log('Uses string .length:', usesLength);
const ascii = 'x'.repeat(200000);
const multibyte = '\u00E9'.repeat(200000);
console.log('ASCII 200K chars:     .length=' + ascii.length + ',   bytes=' + Buffer.byteLength(ascii, 'utf8'));
console.log('Multibyte 200K chars: .length=' + multibyte.length + ', bytes=' + Buffer.byteLength(multibyte, 'utf8'));
console.log('With old .length check, multibyte 200K would pass cap (200000 < 256000): BUG');
console.log('With new byte check, multibyte 200K exceeds cap (400000 > 256000): FIXED');
"
```

Step 3. Ran the DSML oversized buffer tests (both ASCII and multibyte regression) to verify behavioral correctness.

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched build:

Production bundle now uses `Buffer.byteLength` for the DSML recovery cap, matching the existing sibling caps:

```console
$ grep -n 'MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES\|MAX_TOOL_CALL_ARGUMENT_BUFFER\|MAX_POST_TOOL_CALL_BUFFER' dist/openai-transport-stream-DRlicd4V.js
2841:const MAX_POST_TOOL_CALL_BUFFER_BYTES = 256e3;
2842:const MAX_TOOL_CALL_ARGUMENT_BUFFER_BYTES = 256e3;
3189:const MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES = 256e3;
```

```console
$ grep -n 'Buffer.byteLength.*MAX_DSML' dist/openai-transport-stream-DRlicd4V.js
3226:if (final || Buffer.byteLength(buffer, "utf8") > MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES) {
```

Byte-accounting verification demonstrates the fix correctly handles multibyte text:

```console
$ node -e "<proof script above>"
DSML recovery buffer cap: 256000 bytes
Uses Buffer.byteLength: true
Uses string .length: false

ASCII 200K chars:     .length=200000,   bytes=200000
Multibyte 200K chars: .length=200000, bytes=400000

With old .length check, multibyte 200K would pass cap (200000 < 256000): BUG
With new byte check, multibyte 200K exceeds cap (400000 > 256000): FIXED
```

Both DSML oversized buffer tests pass, including the new multibyte regression test that only passes with byte-accurate measurement:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts -t "discards oversized DeepSeek DSML"
 ✓ |agents-core| openai-transport-stream.test.ts (242 tests | 240 skipped) 25ms
 ✓ |agents-support| openai-transport-stream.test.ts (242 tests | 240 skipped) 24ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  4 passed | 480 skipped (484)
```

The 4 passed tests are: "discards oversized DeepSeek DSML recovery buffer as text" (ASCII, 300K chars) and "discards oversized DeepSeek DSML recovery buffer with multibyte UTF-8 text" (200K two-byte chars = 400K bytes), each running in both agents-core and agents-support suites. The multibyte test verifies the cap correctly rejects a payload whose `.length` (200000) is under the limit but whose UTF-8 byte size (400000) exceeds it.

- **Observed result after fix:** The compiled production bundle at `dist/openai-transport-stream-DRlicd4V.js:3226` uses `Buffer.byteLength(buffer, "utf8")` instead of `buffer.length` for the DSML recovery cap. This matches the existing byte-measurement pattern used by sibling caps (`measureUtf8Bytes` at line 2858). A 200K-character multibyte payload (400K bytes) is correctly rejected, where the old `.length` check would have allowed it through.

- **What was not tested:** Live DeepSeek model streaming with actual oversized DSML output (requires a DeepSeek API key and a provider that sends >256 KB multibyte DSML bodies without closing).

target.body:
## Summary
- persist Discord inbound replay claims per account so retryable worker failures can recover without dropping the inbound event
- wire the Discord provider state dir into the replay guard and keep queue commit/release paths on the same replay namespace
- add structured inbound outcome logging and focused tests for duplicate-before-queue, mention gating, and configured binding unavailability

## Verification
- corepack pnpm --dir \"/root/.openclaw/workspace/projects/openclaw-upstream\" check:changed
- env -C \"/root/.openclaw/workspace/projects/openclaw-upstream\" node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.acp-bindings.test.ts
- env -C \"/root/.openclaw/workspace/projects/openclaw-upstream\" node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts
