- env -C \"/root/.openclaw/workspace/projects/openclaw-upstream\" node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo
- env -C \"/root/.openclaw/workspace/projects/openclaw-upstream\" node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior addressed: Discord inbound retries can lose replay state after retryable worker failures, and skipped/duplicate paths are hard to distinguish in logs.
- Real environment tested: Local source checkout with focused Discord tests and extension typecheck.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Re-ran the focused Discord vitest suites plus extension and extension-test tsgo checks listed above.
- Evidence after fix: Replay claims now persist under the OpenClaw state dir per Discord account, duplicate-before-queue drops emit a structured outcome log, and preflight skip/binding-unavailable paths emit structured outcome logs covered by tests.
- Observed result after fix: Focused Discord tests pass, extension typechecks pass, and the branch stays scoped to the Discord inbound replay and diagnostics patch.
- What was not tested: Live Discord round-trip against a real gateway session.

target.title: fix(openai): allow RFC 2544 fake-IP range for Realtime session requests
target.body: ## Summary - `fetchWithSsrFGuard` in `createOpenAIRealtimeSecret` was missing a fake-IP SSRF policy, causing the SSRF guard to block OpenAI Realtime session-secret requests when proxy stacks such as Clash, Surge, or sing-box in TUN/fake-IP mode resolve `api.openai.com` to special-use addresses. - The fix reuses `ssrfPolicyFromHttpBaseUrlFakeIpHostnameAllowlist("https://api.openai.com/v1")`, so the relaxation is still scoped to the expected OpenAI hostname. - Added policy assertions to the OpenAI Realtime voice and transcription tests to prevent silent regression. Closes part of #56535. ## Verification ```bash pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check CI=true pnpm test extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts extensions/openai/realtime-transcription-provider.test.ts ``` - `plugin-sdk:api:check`: passed - Test files: 2 passed - Tests: 59 passed ## Real Behavior Proof - Behavior addressed: SSRF guard blocks OpenAI Realtime session-secret requests when a TUN/fake-IP proxy resolves `api.openai.com` to `198.18.0.0/15`. - Real environment tested: macOS 15, OpenClaw 2026.5.25 (`e3b9c11`), Clash TUN/fake-IP mode active. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=~/.openclaw-dev pnpm gateway:dev --port 19001`; open `http://127.0.0.1:19001`; click Realtime Voice. - Evidence after fix: Browser DevTools console screenshot showing `talk.client.create` returned `ok: true` with `durationMs: 1711`; audio streaming stayed active with continuous `talk.session.appendAudio` calls returning `ok: true`; AI responded in realtime. Screenshot: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/87a884d2-2902-4915-ade1-99993691d5da - Observed result after fix: Realtime voice session fully established, with no SSRF block error. - What was not tested: live Surge / sing-box environments. They use the same code path and policy opt-in. **Browser DevTools — session creation (`talk.client.create` -> `ok: true`):**

target.title: fix(telegram): topic replies stall after session conflicts
target.body:
## Summary

Dead-letter Telegram spooled replays that fail with a reply-session initialization conflict, so one stale replay cannot keep retrying forever and block later updates on the same lane.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users replying in a Telegram forum topic could see live sessions pause or appear stuck when an older spooled replay hit a reply-session initialization conflict. The failed replay was kept pending for retry, so it could re-enter the same permanent failure loop instead of letting later same-topic updates drain.

## Root Cause

Before this change, the Telegram spool replay path only treated missing agent harness failures and dispatch-dedupe rollback failures as non-retryable. Redacted live Gateway logs showed a different permanent replay shape: the replay failed with `reply session initialization conflicted for <redacted session route>` and then logged `spooled update <redacted id> failed; keeping for retry` repeatedly. Because that conflict did not match the non-retryable classifier, the spool retained the stale update and retried it instead of moving it aside.

## Why This Change Was Made

The Telegram polling session now classifies reply-session initialization conflicts as non-retryable spool failures alongside the existing permanent replay cases. That keeps the boundary in the spool replay owner, preserves normal retry behavior for transient failures, and avoids adding any session-store compatibility path or timeout-specific behavior.

## User Impact

Telegram forum-topic sessions can recover from this stale replay state automatically: the conflicting replay is moved to failed spool storage, and later updates in the topic can continue processing instead of sitting behind the same retry loop.

## Real Behavior Proof

Before: redacted live logs showed the same spooled Telegram replay repeatedly failing with a reply-session initialization conflict and being kept for retry.

After: the new regression test writes a stale same-topic replay followed by a later same-topic update. The stale replay is dead-lettered with `reply-session-init-conflict`, pending spool becomes empty, and the later same-topic update runs.

## Evidence

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts -t "dead-letters (missing harness failures|reply session init conflicts|wrapped missing harness failures|grammY BotError-wrapped missing harness failures|buffered spooled claims when dispatch dedupe rollback fails)"

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       5 passed | 58 skipped (63)
```

```text
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts

All matched files use the correct format.
```

```text
git diff --check

(no output)
```

```text
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local

autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct (0.83)
```

## Verification

Focused spool replay regression coverage passed after formatting. The validation intentionally stayed scoped to `extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts` and `extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts` because the change is a narrow classifier addition in the existing Telegram spool failure resolver.

## What Was Not Tested

I did not run the full extension suite or a live Telegram replay against production credentials from this branch. The public PR also omits private Telegram links, chat IDs, message IDs, and raw session identifiers from the live proof.

target.title: feat: add bundled OpenClaw issue reporter skill
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Adds a bundled `openclaw-issue-reporter` skill so fresh OpenClaw installs can draft high-signal issue reports out of the box.

Issue #96511 asks for an issue/PR-writing workflow that improves maintainer intake. This PR intentionally addresses only the issue-reporting half. Issue intake and PR execution have different trigger, safety, and approval boundaries: issue reporting should be available when something is broken; PR execution should remain a stricter workflow after a fix boundary exists.

## Why This Belongs In Bundled Skills

OpenClaw's current direction is right: optional skills should usually ship through ClawHub, and core should stay lean. This PR is meant as a narrow exception to that rule, not a reversal of it.

The reason is that issue reporting is a baseline support and recovery affordance:

- A user reaches for it when OpenClaw is confusing, degraded, newly installed, or broken.
- Asking that user to first discover, trust, install, and invoke a ClawHub skill adds friction at exactly the moment the product needs clean intake. In the concrete failure mode this PR targets, `openclaw skills install`, ClawHub reachability, skill trust verification, or the user's own broken model/provider/channel setup may be part of what they are trying to report.
- Maintainers and bots benefit only if this workflow is reliably present when users report bugs, setup failures, regressions, and plugin/provider issues.
- External coding agents may also be inspecting a broken OpenClaw setup; a bundled plain-text skill is still useful even when the local runtime or ClawHub install path is part of the failure.

So the product argument is not "this is a useful optional skill." It is: OpenClaw should ship one small, conservative, always-available issue intake path because better first reports reduce maintainer back-and-forth and make fresh-user failures actionable.

The proposed bundle criterion is narrow: baseline recovery/support UX that should work before ClawHub discovery, install, verification, or local model/channel health can be trusted belongs in the bundled set. This criterion should not cover PR authoring, team templates, custom maintainer pipelines, automation, diagnostics collectors, or richer workflows; those remain ClawHub-owned.

## Existing Paths Checked

The current tree already has useful adjacent surfaces, but they do not cover this exact always-available intake job:

- `github` / `gh-issues` skills help with GitHub operations, but they do not provide an OpenClaw-specific issue-reporting contract, evidence ladder, redaction checklist, duplicate-search shape, or proof-gap language.
- Gateway diagnostics docs and commands can produce bug-report evidence for gateway failures, but they are component-specific diagnostics, not a general issue-drafting workflow for setup, model/provider, plugin, skill, channel, docs, and feature reports.
- ClawHub search shows generic issue/bug-reporting skills such as `issue-writer`, `issue-triage`, and `qa-bug-reporting`, but those are community/general-purpose skills. They are not first-party, bundled, OpenClaw-specific, or available before `openclaw skills install` and ClawHub trust/verification have succeeded.
- The ClawHub `openclaw` result inspected during this review is unrelated to OpenClaw issue intake; it is a secure key-management skill.

ClawHub remains the right place for richer custom workflows, team-specific intake templates, and PR-authoring workflows. It is weaker as the only answer for first-report quality because it depends on extra discovery/install/trust steps at the moment the user may already be stuck.

## Scope

This PR adds instructions and a template only:

- 2 files
- 322 inserted lines
- no runtime tool
- no diagnostics collector script
- no GitHub write automation
- no ClawHub policy change
- no package/dependency change
- no config/default change
- no PR-authoring workflow

The skill defaults to draft-only. It can gather read-only evidence, search for duplicates, redact private data, and prepare a concise issue draft. Posting or commenting on GitHub still requires explicit approval for the exact final public text.

Future expansion should move back to ClawHub unless it preserves this same guardrail: minimal, draft-only, first-party recovery/support intake with no writes, no collector, no automation, and no PR execution.

## User Impact

Users can ask their agent to report an OpenClaw issue and get a structured, redacted draft with:

- issue type
- impact
- environment
- reproduction steps
- expected vs actual behavior
- selected evidence
- similar issues searched
- unknowns and proof gaps

For non-technical users, the skill asks a few plain questions instead of dumping a command list. For maintainers, the output should reduce repeated follow-up questions about repro steps, version, install path, logs, duplicate search, and what was already ruled out.

## Maintainer Review Requested

This is a product-scope decision, so a maintainer look would be useful even if automation flags the change as "better for ClawHub."

The review question is:

Should OpenClaw bundle one minimal issue-reporting skill as baseline support UX, while leaving richer/custom issue and PR workflows to ClawHub?

If maintainers prefer not to bundle it, the next best alternative is to add a first-party docs pointer or onboarding hook that makes an issue-reporting ClawHub skill discoverable before users hit failures. I think the bundled version is stronger because it is available before any extra install path succeeds.

## Evidence

- Added `skills/openclaw-issue-reporter/SKILL.md`.
- Added `skills/openclaw-issue-reporter/references/issue-template.md`.
- Verified branch-local skill contract with a local `python3` check: frontmatter shape, skill name, description length, reference-template existence, and expected template sections.
- Verified text file sanity: newline endings and no CR bytes.
- Ran `git diff --check HEAD~1 HEAD`.
- Ran local `codex review --base HEAD~1` through the repaired OpenClaw Codex review path.
  - First pass found a security-process issue: vulnerability reports must not be drafted as public GitHub issues/comments with sensitive details.
  - Accepted and fixed that finding by routing `security` reports through private disclosure guidance from `SECURITY.md`.
  - Second pass found no concrete regression or actionable defect.
- Ran focused Codex critiques against the final skill wording after reviewer feedback about trigger words, ClawSweeper follow-up, visual evidence, fresh-agent audience, agent-owned search, selective existing-issue comments, GitHub Security routing, and keeping PR-flow language out of the skill. Final verdict: `STRONG`.

Notes:

- `pnpm exec prettier --check ...` could not be used because the repo did not expose a `prettier` binary after dependency install.
- `openclaw skills check` from the installed local CLI inspected the active local OpenClaw workspace rather than this temporary upstream checkout, so it was not treated as branch proof.

Related: #96511

target.title: fix(agents): broaden local model lean profile
target.body:
## Summary
- Broaden `localModelLean` so small/local models avoid heavyweight web, media, session orchestration, node, channel, and subagent tool families.
- Keep core coding/status tools available in lean mode and cover the retained/trimmed surface with focused tests.
- Refresh config help and local-model docs so the operator-facing contract matches the broader lean profile.

## Verification
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/concepts/experimental-features.md docs/gateway/local-models.md docs/providers/ollama.md`
- `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/concepts/experimental-features.md docs/gateway/local-models.md docs/providers/ollama.md`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/local-model-lean.ts src/agents/local-model-lean.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.model-provider-collision.test.ts src/config/schema.help.ts`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/agents/local-model-lean.ts src/agents/local-model-lean.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.model-provider-collision.test.ts src/config/schema.help.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/local-model-lean.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.model-provider-collision.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/plugins-runtime-boundary.test.ts` - 5 files / 55 tests passed.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sessions/extensions/loader.test.ts` - 2 files / 4 tests passed, covering the previously failed `checks-node-agentic-agents` loader surface.
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` - clean, no accepted/actionable findings.
- AWS Crabbox `check:changed`: provider `aws`, lease `cbx_e10934f873f7`, slug `harbor-shrimp`, run `run_3cdcf7eb920b`, exit `0`, cleanup `leaseStopped=true`.

## Rebase note
The previous `checks-node-agentic-agents` failure was stale-base drift around `@openclaw/net-policy/ip` resolution in `src/agents/sessions/extensions/loader.test.ts`, not this PR's lean-profile diff. Rebased onto `origin/main` `8c0aaee88237e3ac98c63df222c3762972b8a92e`; the exact loader test and the AWS Crabbox changed gate now pass.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: `localModelLean` previously trimmed only `browser`, `cron`, and `message`, which left many large web/media/channel/orchestration schemas visible to smaller local models.
Real environment tested: AWS Crabbox Linux runner, provider `aws`, lease `cbx_e10934f873f7`, run `run_3cdcf7eb920b`, branch head `23e8dabab06c6367aab951b8ceab7917fed99781`, changed gate from base `8c0aaee88237e3ac98c63df222c3762972b8a92e`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: rebased the branch to current main, kept the diff to the 7 intended lean-profile files, checked docs/format/lint, ran the focused lean/profile/config Vitest set locally, ran the previously failing loader test locally, ran autoreview, and ran remote `pnpm check:changed` through AWS Crabbox.
Evidence after fix: Crabbox changed gate completed with exit `0`; focused local Vitest reported 5 files / 55 tests passed; loader test reported 2 files / 4 tests passed; autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings; docs formatter and MDX checks passed.
Observed result after fix: lean mode keeps `read`, `write`, `edit`, `exec`, `apply_patch`, `process`, `session_status`, and `update_plan`, while trimming heavyweight browser/web/media/channel/session/subagent surfaces.
What was not tested: live Ollama or hosted-provider calls in this PR; this PR changes local lean tool visibility and validates the changed code/docs paths.

Related: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/86599

target.title: Repair active npm host after plugin installs
target.body:
Summary
- Detect when a managed plugin npm install prunes dependencies from the active global-prefix OpenClaw package.
- Repair the active OpenClaw package at the same installed version before reporting plugin install success, including required dependencies and optional runtime dependencies that were present before the install.
- Roll back plugin installs if active-host repair fails, and keep npm lifecycle scripts disabled during repair.

Verification
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/install.npm-spec.test.ts src/infra/npm-managed-root.test.ts
- ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/npm-managed-root.ts src/infra/npm-managed-root.test.ts src/plugins/install.ts src/plugins/install.npm-spec.test.ts
- git diff --check
- Testbox-through-Crabbox: tbx_01kspspe3ktdsxrpmg7b24aqtv

Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Installing an external plugin into the same managed npm prefix as a global OpenClaw package can leave the active OpenClaw package missing runtime dependencies.
Real environment tested: Blacksmith Testbox through Crabbox with a PR-built OpenClaw tarball installed into a throwaway global npm prefix, then the published Twilio WhatsApp plugin installed into that same managed prefix.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built and packed this branch, installed the tarball globally with npm --prefix, ran openclaw plugins install npm:@srinathh/openclaw-channel-twilio-whatsapp@2.1.8 --dangerously-force-unsafe-install, verified active host dependencies, then ran openclaw doctor --fix.
Evidence after fix: The Testbox run printed json5=2.2.3, croner=10.0.1, listed twilio-whatsapp in plugins, and ended with PROOF_OK active host deps survived plugin install and doctor ran.
Observed result after fix: Testbox-through-Crabbox tbx_01kspspe3ktdsxrpmg7b24aqtv completed with exit=0. Focused local tests also passed: 2 test files, 58 tests.
What was not tested: The production gateway was not restarted or repointed to this PR. AWS Crabbox did not execute because AWS lease creation hit a security-group rule limit, so the remote proof used Blacksmith Testbox through Crabbox.

target.title: fix(memory): increase QMD embedTimeoutMs default to 600s for local GG…
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Local GGUF models (e.g., embeddinggemma-300m) take 3-4 minutes to embed a 30-50 file workspace on commodity hardware (4-core CPU), but `memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs` default is only 120s, causing repeated timeout failures and effectively disabling vector search.
- Solution: Increase `DEFAULT_QMD_EMBED_TIMEOUT_MS` from 120_000 to 600_000 to match built-in engine's `embeddingBatchTimeoutSeconds` local-provider default. Improve error message to surface config path: `memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs`.
- What changed: 3 files — `backend-config.ts` (default change), `qmd-manager.ts` (error message), `backend-config.test.ts` (test expectation).
- What did NOT change: No runtime behavior change beyond timeout threshold. No config schema change. No API surface change.

## Change Type (select all)
 
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #74204
- Related #60856 (Ollama embeddings timeout, already fixed)
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

The current 120s default is insufficient for local GGUF workloads, while built-in engine correctly uses 600s for local/self-hosted providers. Users discover the fix (`embedTimeoutMs: 600000`) only via `openclaw config schema` or docs, and error message does not hint at the config path. This PR aligns defaults and surfaces the fix in the error message.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Increase QMD embed timeout default from 120s to 600s; improve error message to mention config path.
- Real environment tested: Linux (x86_64), Node 22, local OpenClaw checkout on `fix/qmd-embed-timeout-74204` branch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

grep -n "DEFAULT_QMD_EMBED_TIMEOUT_MS" packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.ts

grep -n "qmd embed failed" extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.test.ts -t "embedTimeoutMs"

- Evidence after fix:

```
$ grep -n "DEFAULT_QMD_EMBED_TIMEOUT_MS" packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.ts
102:const DEFAULT_QMD_EMBED_TIMEOUT_MS = 600_000;

$ grep -n "qmd embed failed" extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts
1800:    log.warn(
1801:      `qmd embed failed (${reason}): ${String(err)} — consider increasing memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs (default: 600000); backing off for ${Math.ceil(delayMs / 1000)}s`,
1802:    );

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.test.ts -t "embedTimeoutMs"
✓ backend-config.test.ts (1 test) 1 passed
```

- Observed result after fix: Default changed from 120_000 to 600_000. Error message now includes config path hint. Test expectation updated and passed.
- What was not tested: Full gateway startup with QMD GGUF embedding on real workspace (no GGUF model available locally).

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — config default mismatch, not a bug in runtime logic.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — default change, no behavior regression risk.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users with local GGUF embedding will see fewer timeout failures after upgrade. Error message now hints at config path if timeout still occurs.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```
Before: DEFAULT_QMD_EMBED_TIMEOUT_MS = 120_000
After:  DEFAULT_QMD_EMBED_TIMEOUT_MS = 600_000

Error message before: "qmd embed failed (...) — backing off for ..."
Error message after:  "qmd embed failed (...) — consider increasing memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs (default: 600000); backing off for ..."
```

## Security Impact (required)

- [ ] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [ ] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [ ] New/changed network calls? No
- [ ] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [ ] Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux x86_64
- Runtime/container: Node 22
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: QMD memory
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Checkout `fix/qmd-embed-timeout-74204` branch
2. Verify default changed via grep
3. Verify error message updated via grep
4. Run test expectation

### Expected

`DEFAULT_QMD_EMBED_TIMEOUT_MS` is 600_000. Error message mentions `memory.qmd.update.embedTimeoutMs`. Test passes.

### Actual

All checks pass as expected.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets (grep output)
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Default value change, error message update, test expectation alignment
- Edge cases checked: N/A (simple config change)
- What you did NOT verify: Live gateway startup with QMD GGUF embedding

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes
- [ ] Config/env changes? No (default only)
- [ ] Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

None — simple default increase aligning with built-in engine's local provider default.

Fixes #74204

target.title: Fix/heartbeat task due clock skew
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `isTaskDue` silently fails when system clock moves backward (NTP correction, VM resume, timezone change). `nowMs - lastRunMs` becomes negative so the task never runs.
- Why it matters: Heartbeat tasks get permanently stuck until the clock catches up past `lastRunMs + interval`, which could be 30+ minutes with no error or warning.
- What changed: Added a `lastRunMs > nowMs` guard that treats a future `lastRunMs` the same as `undefined` (never run).
- What did NOT change: All existing behavior for normal clock progression is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)
- Root cause: `isTaskDue` computes `nowMs - lastRunMs` without checking if `lastRunMs` is in the future. A backward clock jump makes this negative, so the condition `>= intervalMs` is never true.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No guard for `lastRunMs > nowMs` (clock skew scenario).
- Contributing context (if known): Can happen on any system after NTP sync, VM resume, or manual clock correction.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `isTaskDue` returns `true` when `lastRunMs > nowMs`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Pure function, no dependencies, single condition check.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — `isTaskDue` had zero tests before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — test added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
Heartbeat tasks now resume immediately after a clock correction instead of being stuck for up to `interval` minutes.

## Diagram (if applicable)
```text
Before:
[clock skew] -> lastRunMs=future -> nowMs - lastRunMs < 0 -> task stuck forever

After:
[clock skew] -> lastRunMs > nowMs -> treat as never-run -> task runs immediately
```

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A

### Steps
1. Set `lastRunMs` to a future timestamp (simulating clock skew)
2. Call `isTaskDue(futureMs, "30m", nowMs)`
3. Observe result

### Expected
- `true` (task should run)

### Actual (before fix)
- `false` (task permanently stuck)

## Evidence
- [x] Failing test before + passing after (new unit tests added)

## Human Verification (required)
- Verified scenarios: clock skew (lastRunMs in future), normal elapsed interval, interval not yet elapsed, undefined lastRunMs
- Edge cases checked: lastRunMs exactly equal to nowMs (returns false, correct — not due yet)
- What you did NOT verify: actual NTP correction on live system

## Review Conversations
- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: Tasks that were intentionally deferred by setting a future lastRunMs would now run immediately.
  - Mitigation: This is not a supported use case — lastRunMs is only ever set to the current time when a task runs.

target.title: feat(ui): add KaTeX math rendering support
target.body:
## Summary

Add KaTeX-based mathematical formula rendering to OpenClaw chat interface. When `ui.mathRendering` is set to `"katex"`, LaTeX math expressions (both inline `$...$` and display `$$...$$`) are rendered as beautifully formatted math in chat messages.

## Changes

### Configuration
- Add `mathRendering?: "off" | "katex"` to `types.openclaw.ts` UI config
- Add corresponding Zod schema in `zod-schema.ts` (inside `.strict()` ui object)
- Add `ui.mathRendering` label in `schema.labels.ts`, help text in `schema.help.ts`, documentation in `configuration-reference.md`

### Core Rendering
- New `ui/src/ui/katex-renderer.ts` module with math extraction, pre-processing, and synchronous rendering
- Placeholder-based architecture: extract math → sanitize HTML → restore rendered math
- Handles `\$` escaped delimiters, nested braces, display/inline modes
- Post-processes `katex-error` class → falls back to raw LaTeX text
- CJK boundary support: `isCJKBoundary()` covers fullwidth punctuation, CJK punctuation, CJK ideographs, Hangul, Kana
- Markdown formatting boundary support: `*`, `_`, `~` after closing `$` recognized as valid boundaries

### DOMPurify Integration
- New `toSanitizedMarkdownHtmlWithKatex()` function (original `toSanitizedMarkdownHtml` unchanged)
- `uponSanitizeAttribute` DOMPurify hook using `node.closest(".katex")` for attribute preservation
- Extended allowed tags: 29 MathML + SVG tags (msqrt, mroot, msubsup, menclose, etc.)
- Extended allowed attributes: 36 MathML + SVG attributes (mathvariant, displaystyle, preserveAspectRatio, viewBox, etc.)

### Rendering Pipeline
- `grouped-render.ts`: Thread `mathRendering` through `renderMarkdownContent` selector and `renderStreamingGroup`
- Streaming: `mathRendering` passed through `renderStreamingGroup()` → `renderGroupedMessage()`
- `chat.ts`: Preload KaTeX module + CSS when `mathRendering === "katex"` (one-shot guard to prevent render loops)
- 7-file bootstrap chain: `control-ui-contract.ts` → `control-ui.ts` → `control-ui-bootstrap.ts` → `app-view-state.ts` → `app.ts` → `app-render.ts` → `chat.ts`

### Tests
- 87 test cases in `katex-renderer.test.ts` covering extraction, rendering, escaping, error handling, CJK boundaries, Markdown formatting boundaries, maxSize cap, XSS protection
- All UI tests pass, pre-existing failures in googlechat/matrix extensions are unrelated to this PR

### Dependencies
- Pinned `katex@0.16.47` (exact version, zero behavioral impact)

## Design Decisions

- **Full synchronous architecture**: No async changes to `toSanitizedMarkdownHtml`; new sync `toSanitizedMarkdownHtmlWithKatex` function
- **Streaming safety**: KaTeX rendering now flows through streaming path via `mathRendering` parameter
- **CJK boundary support**: Closing `$` before fullwidth punctuation, CJK characters, Hangul, and Kana is recognized as valid boundary
- **Markdown formatting boundary support**: Closing `$` before `*`, `_`, `~` (bold, italic, strikethrough markers) is recognized as valid boundary — fixes `**$x^2$**` not rendering
- **Error resilience**: KaTeX parse errors gracefully fall back to displaying raw LaTeX; `maxSize: 100` prevents layout-breaking output
- **Render loop guard**: `katexPreloadTriggered` one-shot boolean prevents infinite update cycles
- **Zero breakage**: All existing callers of `toSanitizedMarkdownHtml` continue working unchanged

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: KaTeX math rendering in OpenClaw Control UI chat. Inline and display LaTeX formulas render correctly. CJK boundary formulas like `$x^2$的值` and `$E=mc^2$，` render correctly. Markdown formatting boundary formulas like `**$2^{m \times n}$**` and `*$x^2$*` render correctly. Numeric inline math like `$5 and $10$` no longer rejected by currency regex.

**Real environment tested**: OpenClaw Control UI dev server (`pnpm dev:ui:mock`) with `mathRendering: "katex"` enabled, tested in Chrome browser on Linux. Also verified on live OpenClaw gateway (systemd service, port 18789) with production config.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. Set `mathRendering: "katex"` in config
2. Start OpenClaw (dev: `pnpm dev:ui:mock` or gateway: `pnpm gateway:watch`)
3. Open Chrome, navigate to Control UI
4. Send chat messages containing LaTeX formulas:
   - Basic: `$E=mc^2$`, `$$\int_0^1 f(x)dx$$`
   - CJK boundary: `$x^2$的值`, `$E=mc^2$，以及`
   - Markdown formatting: `**$2^{m \times n}$**`, `*$x^2$*`, `~~$a+b$~~`
   - Numeric content: `$5 and $10$`
5. Observe rendered output in chat window — all formulas render correctly

**Evidence after fix**: Screenshots from real OpenClaw UI showing correct rendering:

Inline and display math rendering:
![Inline and display math](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/85a4a034-b275-4510-8d19-f66b9cf4b03e)

Complex formulas (matrices, integrals, nested):
![Complex formulas](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2a75c1c0-50de-4452-a862-fd16e2d27936)

CJK boundary formulas (fullwidth comma, CJK ideograph):
![CJK boundary formulas](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/98b80524-6179-4e3a-af53-7f36ea766e51)

Additional formula rendering (fractions, roots, Greek letters):
![Additional formulas](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/650da05c-14e0-4b5d-ab21-79022feadcfb)

**Observed result after fix**: All LaTeX formulas render correctly in the OpenClaw chat interface — inline math, display math, complex nested formulas, CJK boundary cases, and Markdown formatting boundary cases. KaTeX CSS loads correctly, no raw LaTeX visible, no red error messages, no layout issues. Numeric inline math (e.g. `$5 and $10$`) no longer rejected by false currency detection.

**What was not tested**: Actual AI model integration (requires API key). Multi-session switching while KaTeX is loaded. Very long formula chains that might trigger maxSize fallback.

target.title: fix(discord): account-scoped voice groups + delayed autoJoin safety net
target.body:
## Summary
- Scopes `@discordjs/voice` connections by account ID (`group: "openclaw:<accountId>"`) to prevent multi-bot connection collisions
- Delays initial autoJoin by 6s so voice gateway state settles after READY/RESUMED before joining
- Adds 8s and 75s delayed autoJoin safety nets for cases where voice state is still transient
- Adds `.unref()` on all autoJoin timers so they don't prevent process exit

## Motivation
When multiple bot accounts share a Discord guild, `@discordjs/voice` stores connections by `(group, guildId)` with default group `"default"`. Without account scoping, bots overwrite each other's voice connections (cherry, butler, scholar all share the same guild).

READY/RESUMED gateway events fire before voice server/state info has fully arrived, so autoJoin called immediately fails with "unknown voice state". A 6s delay + retry at 8s/75s closes this window.

Cherry is the 3rd account in a 3-account setup (20s startup stagger). Without multi-retry, she often misses the READY event window entirely and never joins voice.

## Behavior proof

### Before-patch symptom

Multi-account setup (cherry, butler, scholar) on the same guild:

```
# gateway.log — before patch
[discord voice] autoJoin failed account=butler: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'guildId')
# cherry silently never joined — READY event window missed entirely
```

Butler was overwriting cherry's voice state (shared `"default"` group). Cherry's 3rd-account stagger meant she often missed the original autoJoin trigger window.

### After-patch behavior

```
# gateway.log — 2026-05-28, after patch applied via reapply-openclaw-discord-patches.sh
[discord voice] startAutoJoin scheduled 6000ms account=cherry
[discord voice] startAutoJoin scheduled 6000ms account=butler
[discord voice] startAutoJoin fired after 6048ms account=cherry
[discord voice] startAutoJoin settled account=cherry
[discord voice] 8s autoJoin fired after 8031ms account=cherry
[discord voice] 75s autoJoin safety net fired after 75052ms account=butler
```

Each account has an independent voice group (`openclaw:cherry`, `openclaw:butler`). The 75s safety net caught butler's late join after her voice state arrived. No collisions.

### Lint fix (2026-05-29 update)
`if (!voiceManager?.isEnabled()) return;` → wrapped in braces (`curly` rule).

## Changes
| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `extensions/discord/src/voice/manager.ts` | `startAutoJoin` wraps in 6s `setTimeout(..).unref()`; `resolveVoiceConnectionGroup()` helper; all `getVoiceConnection`/`joinVoiceChannel` calls include `group` param |
| `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.ts` | After voice listener registration, schedule autoJoin retries at 8s and 75s with `.unref()`; single-line if wrapped in braces |

## Test plan
- [x] Lint passes (oxlint, curly rule fixed)
- [x] Multi-account voice groups: each account uses `group: "openclaw:<accountId>"`
- [x] autoJoin timers have `.unref()` (process exit not blocked)
- [ ] Single bot regression: voice autoJoin works as before
- [ ] build-artifacts: `kitchen-sink-rpc-walk.test.ts` failure is pre-existing flaky (unrelated to this PR, see [comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/87527#issuecomment-4569705966))

target.title: Add generic webhooks plugin
target.body:
## Summary

- add a configurable `webhooks` plugin for authenticated inbound webhook routes
- support route-specific auth, event allowlists, idempotency, fast ACK, TaskFlow dispatch, scheduled agent dispatch, delivery-only routes, and completion delivery
- split the webhook ingress implementation into focused modules for auth, event extraction, idempotency, templating, dispatch, delivery, TaskFlow action compatibility, completion delivery, and target wiring
- add Gateway WebSocket-managed dynamic subscriptions with `webhooks.subscribe`, `webhooks.list`, `webhooks.remove`, and `webhooks.test`
- align `openclaw webhooks subscribe/list/remove/test` with Hermes-style operator UX: readable setup output by default, `--json` for scripts, direct URL/secret/event/dispatch hints, and provider POST guidance
- print full dynamic subscription webhook URLs when `plugins.entries.webhooks.config.publicUrl` is configured, while rejecting WebSocket URLs for provider delivery
- keep external provider delivery on standard HTTP webhook POST routes, matching the Hermes-style data-plane model instead of introducing a separate local relay connector
- remove the standalone WebSocket relay connector and its `ws` dependency from the webhooks plugin

## Verification

target.title: fix(bash-tools): default stdin to pipe-closed in service-managed mode
target.body:
## Problem

When OpenClaw runs as a launchd service (`OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MODE=launchd`), spawned child processes inherit fd 0 (stdin) from the service manager process. On Node 26, launchd closes fd 0 before exec. The child process inherits a closed file descriptor; when Node.js tries to set up the inherited stream, it throws `EBADF` (bad file descriptor) during child startup.

## Fix

In `createChildAdapter`, extract `isServiceManagedRuntime()` into a local `serviceManaged` boolean (avoiding two calls), then apply it to the `stdinMode` default:

```ts
const serviceManaged = isServiceManagedRuntime();
const stdinMode =
  params.stdinMode ?? (params.input !== undefined || serviceManaged ? "pipe-closed" : "inherit");
const useDetached = process.platform !== "win32" && !serviceManaged;
```

When `serviceManaged` is `true`, stdin defaults to `"pipe-closed"` (a closed pipe) instead of `"inherit"`. This avoids giving children a closed fd 0 and eliminates the EBADF.

Outside service mode the existing POSIX detached behavior is preserved: `stdinMode` defaults to `"inherit"` for interactive shells and sub-shells.

## Behavior proof

### Before-patch symptom (OpenClaw on macOS launchd, Node 26)

```
[supervisor] spawn error account=cherry
Error: EBADF: bad file descriptor, read
    at ChildProcess.<anonymous> (node:child_process:...)
    at ChildProcess.emit (node:events:...)
```

The error appears immediately on `createChildAdapter` when launchd has closed fd 0 and `stdinMode` defaults to `"inherit"`.

### After-patch behavior

With `serviceManaged = true`, stdin is set to `"pipe-closed"`. The child process never tries to inherit the closed fd. No EBADF on startup; children spawn cleanly.

```
# gateway.log — launchd service boot (2026-05-28)
[gateway] starting account=cherry
[discord] provider started account=cherry
[discord] gateway connected account=cherry
```

The cherry agent connected without any EBADF errors after the patch was applied to the local runtime via `reapply-openclaw-discord-patches.sh`.

## Notes on `checks-node-agentic-control-plane-runtime` CI failure

The failing tests (`server.shared-token-hot-reload.test.ts`, `server.shared-token-session-rotation.test.ts`) are in a different package and not touched by this PR. The failure is a Vitest `EnvironmentTeardownError` in the `gateway-server` test suite — a known intermittent issue unrelated to `child.ts`. See [PR comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/87527#issuecomment-4569705966).

target.title: feat(volcengine): add allowPrivateNetwork option for TTS behind TUN proxy
target.body:
## Summary
Adds `allowPrivateNetwork` boolean option to Volcengine TTS provider to support TUN proxy stacks that use RFC 2544 fake-IP ranges for DNS resolution.

**Security approach (revised from original PR):** Instead of passing the broad `allowPrivateNetwork: true` SSRF flag (which would open all of RFC 1918), this PR uses two narrow policy bits already in `SsrfPolicy`:
- `allowRfc2544BenchmarkRange: true` — opens 198.18.0.0/15 (RFC 2544, the fake-IP range used by Surge/Clash/sing-box)
- `allowIpv6UniqueLocalRange: true` — opens IPv6 ULA (same use case)

Full RFC 1918 space (10.x, 172.16.x, 192.168.x) and metadata addresses remain blocked.

## Motivation
When running behind a TUN proxy (Surge, Clash, sing-box) that uses fake-IP DNS (198.18.0.0/15), the SSRF guard blocks the resolved Volcengine API hostname. This makes TTS fail silently.

## Changes
| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `extensions/volcengine/tts.ts` | Add `allowPrivateNetwork` to both `seedSpeechTTS` and `legacyVolcengineTTS`; spread narrow fake-IP bits conditionally in `policy` |
| `extensions/volcengine/speech-provider.ts` | Read flag from config, propagate through `readProviderConfig`, forward to `volcengineTTS()` |

## Behavior proof

### Real-world reproduction

Cherry voice (OpenClaw STT-TTS) behind Surge TUN proxy on macOS. Surge uses fake-IP DNS; API hostnames resolve to 198.18.x.x. Without `allowPrivateNetwork`, the SSRF guard blocked the outbound TTS call.

After setting `allowPrivateNetwork: true` in the Cherry voice config, TTS resumed:

```
# gateway.log — 2026-05-16
[reload] config change detected (channels.discord.accounts.cherry.voice.tts.providers.volcengine.allowPrivateNetwork)
[reload] config hot reload applied
```

### Unit test proof (2026-05-29 addition — all 4 pass)

New tests in `volcengineTTS > allowPrivateNetwork SSRF policy` (`tts.test.ts`):

```
✓ seed speech: omits fake-IP policy bits when allowPrivateNetwork is false (default)
✓ seed speech: adds narrow fake-IP policy bits when allowPrivateNetwork is true
✓ legacy TTS: omits fake-IP policy bits when allowPrivateNetwork is false (default)
✓ legacy TTS: adds narrow fake-IP policy bits when allowPrivateNetwork is true
```

These tests confirm:
- Default (`false`): `allowRfc2544BenchmarkRange` and `allowIpv6UniqueLocalRange` are NOT present in policy
- Opt-in (`true`): both narrow bits ARE present; the broad `allowPrivateNetwork` flag is NOT present (full RFC 1918 stays blocked)

## Test plan
- [x] Default: SSRF guard blocks fake-IP range (policy does not contain fake-IP bits)  
- [x] Opt-in: only 198.18.0.0/15 + IPv6 ULA opened; RFC 1918 metadata addresses remain blocked
- [x] TypeScript compiles without errors
- [x] Lint passes (oxlint)

target.title: fix(anthropic): configure undici Agent with extended keep-alive to prevent socket failures
target.body:
## What changed

This fix configures Anthropic provider to use a custom undici Agent with extended keep-alive timeout, preventing socket failures on long-running gateways.

**Root cause**: The Anthropic SDK uses undici with default `keepAliveTimeout` of 4 seconds. After 1-3 hours of uptime, idle keep-alive connections get closed by network infrastructure, causing `UND_ERR_SOCKET` errors on subsequent requests. These errors are classified as `timeout` and trigger the fallback chain, silently swapping to a fallback model mid-conversation.

**Solution**: Create a custom undici Agent with:
- `keepAliveTimeout: 60_000` (60s vs default 4s)
- `connectTimeout: 15_000` (15s)
- `bodyTimeout: 600_000` (10min)
- `allowH2: false` (disable HTTP/2 for compatibility)
- `connections: 20` (limit per origin)
- `pipelining: 4` (better throughput)

Apply this Agent to all Anthropic client variants via `fetchOptions.dispatcher`.

## Why

The issue (#87407) reports real production occurrences where users saw "LLM request rejected" errors with internal API path references, and the agent silently switched models mid-turn. This is a poor user experience and makes debugging difficult. By extending keep-alive timeouts, we reduce the chance of socket keep-alive timeouts on established connections, preventing unnecessary fallbacks.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: UND_ERR_SOCKET keep-alive failures causing silent mid-turn fallback to secondary models.

**Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout, Node.js 24.x, TypeScript compilation verified.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Build and type-check
pnpm build:ci-artifacts

# Run focused tests related to Anthropic provider
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts

# Run autoreview (if available)
# /Users/steipete/Projects/agent-scripts/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main
```

**Evidence after fix**: Build succeeds without errors. Anthropic provider tests pass (refer to CI results).

**Observed result after fix**: The Anthropic client now uses a custom undici Agent with extended `keepAliveTimeout` of 60 seconds. This prevents idle socket closures that previously caused `UND_ERR_SOCKET` errors during long-running sessions. The connection pool is configured with conservative timeouts and limits.

**What was not tested**: 
- Live multi-hour uptime stress test 
- Real Anthropic API credential testing (requires production deployment)
- HTTP/2 behavior (explicitly disabled)
- Specific network conditions that caused original failures (requires reproducer access)

**Verification**: This change should be validated in a staging/production environment by monitoring for the absence of `UND_ERR_SOCKET` errors in gateway logs over extended uptime periods when using Anthropic providers.

target.title: [codex] Expire Telegram message-cache plugin state
target.body:
## Summary
- gives Telegram prompt-context message-cache plugin-state rows a seven-day TTL
- normalizes existing no-expiry rows in a TTL-enabled namespace on the next write so upgraded `telegram.message-cache` rows can age out too
- preserves sibling namespace budgets and adds focused coverage for the Telegram message-cache persistent store options

Refs openclaw/openclaw#87332 and openclaw/openclaw#87357.

## Notes
Current `main` already contains the generic plugin-state cap fix from `e3395867507` (`fix(plugin-state): evict current namespace on plugin row cap`). This PR addresses the remaining `telegram.message-cache` permanence angle from #87332.

The follow-up commit also addresses the review concern about pre-existing `expires_at = NULL` rows. When a namespace writes with a TTL, plugin state now updates live rows in that same namespace that were previously immortal to expire at the same deadline as the write. That keeps the upgrade path bounded without touching sibling namespaces.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/message-cache.test.ts -t 'plugin-state store|persistent'`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.test.ts -t 'default TTL|Telegram sibling|plugin row cap|plugin overflow'`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.sqlite.ts src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/message-cache.ts extensions/telegram/src/message-cache.test.ts`

Behavior addressed: Telegram message-cache plugin-state entries now get a TTL instead of being permanent live rows, and old no-expiry message-cache rows receive a bounded expiry after the namespace writes under the new TTL policy.
Real environment tested: local macOS source worktree at `upstream/main`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: focused Vitest and oxfmt commands above.
Evidence after fix: Telegram message-cache tests passed for persistent/default store coverage; plugin-state tests passed for TTL normalization, sibling namespace budget, and plugin cap behavior.
Observed result after fix: default Telegram message-cache plugin state opens with namespace `telegram.message-cache`, max entries `3000`, and a seven-day default TTL; existing no-expiry rows in that namespace are assigned the new expiry deadline on the next TTL write.
What was not tested: live Telegram traffic or a production SQLite file from a real user profile.

target.title: docs(gateway): add optional caffeinate-wrapper note to macOS troubleshooting
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a fourth "What to do" step in the macOS gateway troubleshooting section that just landed via 6727985365, documenting the well-established operator pattern of wrapping the gateway in `caffeinate(8)` to prevent macOS Maintenance Sleep entirely. This is a follow-up to the docs change I co-authored with @steipete in that commit; this PR fills the one gap I deliberately left out of the original prose because I wanted the first change to land cleanly first.

The original change documents three operator-side actions (upgrade to `v2026.5.26`, `pmset` flags, launchd liveness watchdog). All three are useful, but on the host that originally surfaced this symptom, the action that actually eliminated maintenance sleep entirely was wrapping the gateway PID in `caffeinate -s -i -w`. Operators hitting the same pattern are likely to reach for this regardless of whether the docs mention it; explicit guidance with the right caveats is more useful than silence.

No code changes. Docs-only, single file (`docs/gateway/troubleshooting.md`), additive (no removals, no rewording of existing steps).

## What the new step adds

The new step is structured to make the project's stance on `caffeinate` explicit, not implicit:

- **Frames the pattern as out-of-tree.** "OpenClaw does not ship a `caffeinate` wrapper and prior proposals to add one upstream were declined." Avoids any implication that the project endorses this for general operators.
- **Cites prior art from established Mac services.** Plex Media Server's community recipe prepends `/usr/bin/caffeinate -ims --` to the Plex `LaunchAgent` `ProgramArguments` for the same reason. The open-source [`alwaysBeCaffeinating`](https://github.com/thomstratton/alwaysBeCaffeinating) LaunchDaemon documents the more general pattern. Both are linked.
- **Lists both common operator shapes.** A sidecar `LaunchAgent` that watches the gateway PID (works around `openclaw doctor` rewriting the gateway plist), vs the simpler Plex-style `ProgramArguments` wrapper (which gets stomped by `doctor` on every run). Operators can pick the one that matches their tolerance for ongoing maintenance.
- **Calls out the trade-offs.** Effective measurement (zero Maintenance Sleep events / zero stability bundles on the affected host over a ~20-hour window), out-of-scope architectural status linking to the closed prior PRs (#15444, #40846), and the laptop / shared-use-Mac warning that the host will not idle-sleep while the gateway is alive.

## Why a separate PR rather than adding to #87337 originally

I deliberately left this out of the original docs PR (#87337) because:

1. That PR was already on shaky ground with ClawSweeper over a release-consistency wording issue. Adding `caffeinate` to it would have given the bot a second thing to flag and would have stretched a single small docs change into an architectural debate.
2. Prior `caffeinate`-as-OpenClaw-feature PRs (#15444, #40846) had been declined. I wanted to confirm that the maintainers were comfortable with the rest of the troubleshooting prose before testing whether documenting the operator-side pattern (with all the caveats) was also acceptable.
3. Now that the first change is landed and shipped, this PR is just the small additive follow-up: keep the operator pattern visible, but framed correctly.

If maintainers prefer to leave `caffeinate` out entirely on the principle that even documenting it implies endorsement, I will happily close this PR without rework. The original three-step recovery is still complete and useful.

## Changes

- **`docs/gateway/troubleshooting.md`**: Adds a fourth "What to do" item (between the launchd liveness watchdog and the "Related" links) covering the `caffeinate` operator pattern. 13 insertions, 0 deletions.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** On a macOS host running OpenClaw `v2026.5.22`, the gateway process was repeatedly exiting with uncaught `ENETDOWN` exceptions raised from inside the SSRF guard's outbound `connect`. The crashes correlated 1:1 with macOS Maintenance Sleep / Power Nap events that briefly drop `en0` to state 0 for ~2.5 seconds, and were aggravated by launchd's silent-park-after-crash-burst behavior. Symptom from the operator's side: all channels (Telegram, WhatsApp) go silent for minutes to hours at a time, only resuming when the operator opens the Control UI or SSHs into the host (which re-arms launchd's respawn gate). This PR's new docs step documents wrapping the gateway in `caffeinate -s -i -w <pid>` as an out-of-tree operator fix.

**Real environment tested:** Apple Silicon Mac mini (M-series), macOS, OpenClaw `v2026.5.22` initially and `v2026.5.26` after upgrade. Apple `caffeinate(8)` shipped with the OS. Setup is a real sidecar `LaunchAgent` (`ai.chintu.gateway-caffeinate`) running as `gui/$UID` on this host, with a supervisor shell script that tracks the gateway PID via `ps -axo pid=,command= | awk` and respawns `caffeinate` on gateway death. Sidecar has been running continuously since 2026-05-26 ~12:08 PT.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** The patch is docs-only and does not introduce any new behavior to test directly. What I tested was the operator pattern the new step describes, on the host that surfaced the original symptom. The steps mirror what an operator following the new doc section would do: install the caffeinate sidecar LaunchAgent with `launchctl bootstrap gui/$UID ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.chintu.gateway-caffeinate.plist` then `launchctl kickstart gui/$UID/ai.chintu.gateway-caffeinate`; verify caffeinate is now tracking the gateway PID with `ps -ef | grep caffeinate | grep -v grep` and `pmset -g assertions | grep -E "PreventSystemSleep|PreventUserIdleSystemSleep"`; monitor for new ENETDOWN crashes with `ls ~/.openclaw/logs/stability/` and `pmset -g log | grep 'Entering Sleep state'`; check gateway uptime with `launchctl print gui/$UID/ai.openclaw.gateway`; and finally simulate gateway death to exercise the self-healing re-attach by sending `SIGTERM` to the caffeinate process and watching the supervisor log respawn.

**Evidence after fix:** Sampled at 2026-05-27 07:00 PT, after the sidecar had been running continuously for ~19 hours. Terminal output captured live from the affected host:

    $ pmset -g log | grep 'Entering Sleep state' | awk '$1 == "2026-05-27"' | wc -l
    0

    $ ls ~/.openclaw/logs/stability/ | wc -l
    3
    (All three bundles are from 2026-05-25 / 2026-05-26 BEFORE the caffeinate sidecar was active.
     No new uncaught_exception bundles after 2026-05-26 12:08.)

    $ launchctl print gui/$UID/ai.openclaw.gateway | grep -E "state|runs|last exit"
            state = running
            runs = 1
            last exit code = (never exited)

    $ pmset -g assertions | grep -E "PreventSystemSleep|PreventUserIdleSystemSleep" | head -4
       pid 79263(node): [...] PreventSystemSleep    named: "ai.openclaw.gateway"
       pid 79263(node): [...] PreventUserIdleSystemSleep    named: "ai.openclaw.gateway"
       pid <caffeinate-pid>(caffeinate): [...] on behalf of Process ID 79263

Process tree at the time of sampling:

    launchd -> /bin/sh gateway-caffeinate.sh -> /usr/bin/caffeinate -s -i -w <gateway-pid>

I also explicitly tested the self-healing re-attach: killed `caffeinate` with `SIGTERM` to simulate the gateway dying. The sidecar's supervisor log recorded `caffeinate exited (rc=143, gateway PID X is gone); will look for new gateway`, found the new gateway PID after launchd's respawn, and spawned a fresh `caffeinate` within ~2 seconds. This is the live runtime log excerpt the doc step is describing for operators.

**Observed result after fix:** With the caffeinate sidecar active, Maintenance Sleep events on this host went from ~28/day (after the partial `pmset` workaround documented in step 2 of the existing section) to zero across a ~20-hour window. New `uncaught_exception` bundles in `~/.openclaw/logs/stability/` went from 1-3/day pre-fix to zero. Gateway uptime is now continuous (`runs = 1`, last exit code = "never exited"). Channels (Telegram and WhatsApp) have stayed reachable across the entire observed window without operator intervention. Stdout from the supervisor confirms the assertions are held continuously and the re-attach path works on simulated death.

**What was not tested:** I did not test the Plex-style `ProgramArguments` wrapper variant on this host. The new doc step notes that variant gets stomped by `openclaw doctor`; I confirmed `doctor` rewrites the gateway plist by reading the source (`buildLaunchAgentPlist$1` in `dist/launchd-*.js`) but did not actually run the wrapper variant long enough to observe a wipe. I also did not exercise the Mintlify production build for the updated section.

## Related

- Original troubleshooting section (already in `main`): 6727985365, co-authored with @steipete from #87337 / #87356.
- Original issue: #86688.
- Code-level ENETDOWN classifier fix (already in `v2026.5.26`): #86762.
- Prior `caffeinate`-as-built-in proposals that were declined (referenced explicitly in the new step for transparency): #15444 (stale), #40846 (closed).

## AI-assisted disclosure

This PR was drafted with AI assistance. The structure of the new step, the trade-off list, the prior-art citations, and the wording were drafted by an assistant working from the operator's own `caffeinate` sidecar setup (running on the host since 2026-05-26), the existing post-merge prose in `docs/gateway/troubleshooting.md`, and a small amount of community-pattern research on Plex's recipe and `alwaysBeCaffeinating`. The operator (me) reviewed the diff in full, owns the sidecar setup the step describes, and can defend each line on review.

## Maintainer edit access

`Allow edits by maintainers` is enabled on this PR (verified post-create against `/repos/openclaw/openclaw/pulls/<NUM>.maintainer_can_modify`). If a maintainer wants to land the content directly into a sweep commit the way @steipete did for the prior PR (6727985365), they can push directly to this branch.

target.title: feat(cron): surface fallback progress
target.body:
## Summary

- Stop already-admitted model fallback chains from losing their embedded cron fallback candidate when Gateway drain begins; new queue enqueues still reject during drain.
- Carry the drain-continuation state through `LiveSessionModelSwitchError` redirects so later embedded fallback candidates stay admitted after Gateway drain starts.
- Add queue pressure fields to model fallback observations and surface cron primary/fallback progress into detached task run status.
- Sanitize fallback failure detail and persisted cron progress fields before task status/CLI surfaces can read them, including ANSI, OSC, C0, C1, and DEL control-character coverage.
- Strip 8-bit C1 OSC sequences in the shared ANSI helper and remove the full C1 control range in compact console sanitization.
- Replace the undefined docs example with source-backed OpenClaw artifacts (`cron task ledgers` and `command lane snapshots`).
- Rebase onto latest `upstream/main` and keep the inherited migration selection compatibility alias explicitly deprecated so architecture/deprecated-JSDoc passes.
- Thread cron task run ids through manual/isolated execution, add a reproducible runtime proof script, and document the production AI governance checklist.

## Verification

- PASS: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts src/process/command-queue.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.task-progress.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.run-embedded.e2e.test.ts` (4 shards, 133 tests)
- PASS: `pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro/cron-fallback-progress-proof.ts`
- PASS: `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo`
- PASS: `pnpm lint --threads=8`

- PASS: `git diff --check`
- PASS: `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Already-active cron/background work can continue its configured model fallback chain after Gateway drain begins, including the embedded runner's session/global queue shape. New command enqueues still reject during drain. Cron-style task progress records primary/fallback model status plus queue active/queued/draining state, with fallback failure detail and terminal-control-bearing progress fields sanitized before persistence.
- Redirect repair after review: if a `LiveSessionModelSwitchError` redirects an already-started fallback chain to a later candidate after Gateway drain starts, the redirected embedded candidate now receives `allowGatewayDrainingContinuation=true`. The focused embedded e2e covers this by marking drain in the primary attempt, throwing a live-switch redirect to groq, verifying groq succeeds, and verifying new work is still rejected during drain.
- Sanitizer repair after review: C1 OSC (`0x9d ... 0x9c`) is stripped by `stripAnsi`, and compact console sanitization removes the full C1 control range (`0x80`-`0x9f`) before cron progress is persisted. The cron progress regression injects C1 OSC in provider/model/fallback detail and asserts no C1/control bytes or C1 OSC payload remain.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS source checkout at `/private/tmp/openclaw-pr-87343-rebase`; PR head `a894a559da2d5549282e28a08cf05fe7656d1a92`; rebased onto `upstream/main@1a3ce7c2a8da266cd0bb2844d44ba5af3942908b`; proof used isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/var/folders/_x/34p90_ts62zcl8g4bvfkyjsw0000gn/T/openclaw-pr-87343-proof-6OmK7F`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro/cron-fallback-progress-proof.ts`.
- Evidence after fix:

```text

[proof] PASS embedded queue-shaped fallback continued during drain; new enqueue still rejected
```

target.title: fix(tools): treat non-positive gateway timeoutMs as absent
target.body:
## Summary

`resolveGatewayOptions` in `src/agents/tools/gateway.ts` clamps an optional `opts.timeoutMs` via `Math.max(1, Math.floor(opts.timeoutMs))` before falling back to the 30s default. A caller-supplied `0` was rewritten to `1ms` instead of falling back to the default, so the very next gateway call timed out instantly.

This matters in practice because **GPT-5.5 and Codex both fill optional integer fields with `0`** when they have no opinion. Any tool wrapping a gateway call with an optional `timeoutMs` (the WhatsApp `message` / `reaction` tools were the first I hit, but the same shape exists in other channel tools) silently turns real user requests into 1ms timeouts on dispatch.

## Fix

Only honour `timeoutMs` when it is a finite, **strictly positive** number. Non-positive or non-finite values fall through to the 30s default — i.e. treated the same as the field being absent.

```ts
const timeoutMs =
  typeof opts?.timeoutMs === "number" && Number.isFinite(opts.timeoutMs) && opts.timeoutMs > 0
    ? Math.floor(opts.timeoutMs)
    : 30_000;
```

## Tests

Added a regression case in `src/agents/tools/gateway.test.ts`. All 38 cases in the two gateway test files pass locally.

## Production evidence

Carried as a local patch on cluster image `2026.5.19-beta.1-lue.2-timeout-zero` (2026-05-20). After the fix, a real WhatsApp reaction smoke test logged `message.action 1021ms` and returned `ok: true`; before, the same call timed out instantly. Bookkeeping in [`valkyriweb/openclaw-claude` ISSUE-104](https://github.com/valkyriweb/openclaw-claude/blob/main/docs/issues/issue-104.md).

## Tradeoff

None I can see. A `timeoutMs: 0` override is never useful — it cannot succeed. Treating it as absent is the only sensible behaviour. Negative values (currently also clamped to 1ms) get the same fix as a side effect; same argument.

target.title: fix(talk): diagnose invalid OpenAI realtime auth
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Talk configured for OpenAI Realtime could surface a raw provider error such as `Incorrect API key provided` when the available credential was an Azure AI Foundry / Azure OpenAI model key or OpenAI-compatible proxy key rather than a direct OpenAI Platform credential.

Why does this matter now?

- OpenAI Realtime Talk auth is separate from the regular model-provider stack. Users with Azure-backed model configs need a clear diagnostic instead of a misleading low-level OpenAI API-key failure.

What is the intended outcome?

- OpenAI Realtime Talk fails early with an actionable message that explains direct OpenAI Realtime auth, `openai-codex` OAuth, or Azure gateway relay configuration.
- Obvious Azure/proxy-style keys are not sent to the OpenAI Realtime client-secret endpoint.
- Upstream invalid-key responses and pre-ready WebSocket auth failures are translated into the same safe diagnostic without leaking the configured secret.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- This does not add a new Azure Foundry browser WebRTC flow.
- This does not change Google Gemini Live or other Talk providers.
- This does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`.

What does success look like?

- A user with only Azure AI Foundry / Azure OpenAI model credentials no longer sees only a raw `Incorrect API key provided` error when enabling OpenAI Realtime Talk.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The auth-resolution behavior in `extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.ts`, especially preserving valid direct OpenAI Realtime setups and `openai-codex` OAuth fallback.
- The diagnostics avoid leaking key material.

AI-assisted: yes. I used GitHub Copilot CLI to implement and validate this fix, and I understand the resulting code changes.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

- N/A; no public issue exists for this local reproduction.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

- N/A.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- No maintainer request; this is a focused bug fix from a real local Talk/OpenAI Realtime configuration issue.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: OpenAI Realtime Talk with Azure AI Foundry / Azure OpenAI or OpenAI-compatible proxy credentials could expose a raw provider invalid-key error instead of explaining that direct OpenAI Realtime auth is required.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 under WSL, using the same local OpenClaw config directory as my running setup. The setup is Azure-backed for regular model providers and has no valid direct OpenAI Platform Realtime key.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `OPENAI_API_KEY=<redacted proxy key> node --import tsx -e "import { buildOpenAIRealtimeVoiceProvider } from './extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.ts'; const provider = buildOpenAIRealtimeVoiceProvider(); try { await provider.createBrowserSession?.({ providerConfig: {}, instructions: 'diagnostic smoke test' }); console.log('unexpected success'); process.exitCode = 1; } catch (error) { const message = error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error); console.log(message.replace(/REDACTED_KEY_PATTERN/g, '<redacted key>')); console.log('secret leaked:', message.includes(process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY ?? '')); }"`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/talk.test.ts --run`
  - `corepack pnpm check:changed`
  - `git diff --check`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
Config (/home/<user>/.openclaw/openclaw.json): missing env var "GEMINI_API_KEY" at talk.realtime.providers.google.apiKey - feature using this value will be unavailable
Talk Realtime provider 'openai' requires a direct OpenAI API key (or supported OpenAI Realtime auth such as openai-codex OAuth). Azure AI Foundry / Azure OpenAI model credentials and OpenAI-compatible proxy keys are not valid for OpenAI Realtime sessions against api.openai.com. Configure talk.realtime.providers.openai.apiKey or OPENAI_API_KEY with a direct OpenAI Platform key, configure openai-codex OAuth, or use Talk gateway relay with an Azure Realtime deployment (azureEndpoint + azureDeployment).
secret leaked: false
```

Additional local validation output:

```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  96 passed (96)
```

`check:changed` completed with typecheck extensions, typecheck extension tests, lint extensions, media download helper guard, runtime sidecar loader guard, and runtime import cycles all passing. Re-run after both Codex follow-up fixes and the ClawSweeper P1 custom-endpoint fix.

- Observed result after fix: The OpenAI Realtime browser-session path rejects obvious non-direct keys before minting client secrets, preserves OAuth fallback when available, and wraps upstream invalid-key failures into the actionable diagnostic.
- What was not tested: A live OpenAI Realtime WebRTC session with a real OpenAI Platform key, and a live Azure Realtime deployment through gateway relay.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: I do not have a valid direct OpenAI Platform Realtime key or Azure Realtime deployment available in this environment, so live provider calls were not performed. The proof includes a real local OpenClaw source execution of the failing credential class plus targeted regression coverage and repository checks.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The previous behavior was represented by existing tests expecting the raw `Incorrect API key provided` startup failure; this PR updates that behavior to the clearer Talk Realtime auth diagnostic.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `OPENAI_API_KEY=<redacted proxy key> node --import tsx -e "..."`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/talk.test.ts --run`
- `corepack pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added OpenAI Realtime provider tests for obvious proxy keys, Azure-looking keys, configured proxy keys, keychain-resolved proxy keys, OAuth fallback, and upstream invalid-key wrapping.
- Updated the pre-ready WebSocket auth-failure expectation to the new diagnostic.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Users could receive a raw OpenAI provider error such as `Incorrect API key provided: <redacted OpenAI-like key>` when Talk used OpenAI Realtime with a non-direct OpenAI credential.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No migration. There is a diagnostic-only auth behavior change for clearly invalid OpenAI Realtime credentials, plus an escape hatch `OPENCLAW_OPENAI_REALTIME_ALLOW_UNVALIDATED_KEY` for unusual direct-key formats.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Misclassifying an unusual but valid OpenAI Realtime credential as non-direct.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The early rejection is limited to high-confidence proxy/Azure-looking formats and invalid prefixes, valid-looking direct OpenAI keys still reach the existing OpenAI flow, `openai-codex` OAuth is preferred/fallback capable for GPT Realtime models, and an environment escape hatch allows unvalidated keys.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Ready for maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI and maintainer review.
- Maintainers can decide whether the provided local proof is sufficient or if they want live Azure/OpenAI Realtime proof.
- `codex review --base origin/main` was not run because the `codex` CLI is not available in this environment.
- Maintainer edits are enabled on the fork PR.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Addressed ClawSweeper P1 feedback about endpoint-only custom Realtime routes: configured and env proxy-looking keys are preserved for custom `azureEndpoint` routes, with regression tests.
- Documented `OPENCLAW_OPENAI_REALTIME_ALLOW_UNVALIDATED_KEY` as an emergency override instead of leaving it undocumented.
- Addressed Codex feedback about overly broad 403 mapping: non-auth 403 responses from the Realtime client-secret endpoint are now preserved as provider permission errors, and a regression test covers this behavior.
- Addressed Codex feedback about Azure realtime startup auth failures: startup auth rewriting now only applies to direct OpenAI realtime WebSocket sessions, and a regression test preserves Azure deployment 401 errors.

target.title: docs: clarify LanceDB dreaming settings vs vector-native plugins
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a short "LanceDB and dreaming" note to `docs/concepts/dreaming.md`:

- A selected memory-slot plugin may expose dreaming **settings** when it owns `plugins.slots.memory`.
- That is separate from LanceDB-**vector-native** dreaming behavior; for third-party implementations, users can browse community plugins on ClawHub.

Generic only - no package names, install scripts, hooks, release links, or plugin-specific endorsement.

Closes #87535

---

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Docs-only clarification. This PR adds a short note to `docs/concepts/dreaming.md` that distinguishes slot-owned dreaming settings from LanceDB-vector-native dreaming plugins discoverable on ClawHub. No runtime code changes.

Real environment tested: OpenClaw 2026.5.27, Ubuntu 24.04, LanceDB Pro memory slot, 1000+ vectors (bge-m3 @ 1024d), production gateway on Tencent Cloud Lighthouse.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Checked the revised docs diff in `docs/concepts/dreaming.md`.

3. Verified current tests cover `memory-lancedb` owning `plugins.slots.memory`.
4. Verified a real LanceDB setup on OpenClaw 2026.5.27 after upgrade.
